target.title: docs(cli): clarify strict json parsing
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: the CLI docs said values use JSON5 parsing with string fallback, but did not clearly separate the strict JSON mode.
- Why it matters: users trying `--strict-json` need to know it errors instead of silently falling back to a raw string.
- What changed: clarified the docs wording for `--strict-json`, including the legacy `--json` alias and examples for number and array values.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): no CLI behavior changed.

## Change Type (select all)

- [ ] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [x] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [x] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Related to #65345; addresses only the strict JSON docs mismatch and should not close the broader tracker.
- Related #
- [ ] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: `--strict-json` requires JSON parsing and errors on invalid JSON instead of using the raw string fallback.
- Real environment tested: local OpenClaw checkout on Linux, Node v24.13.0, pnpm test runner.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:
  - `openclaw config set gateway.port 19001 --strict-json`
  - `openclaw config set channels.whatsapp.groups '["*"]' --strict-json`
  - `openclaw config set gateway.port '{bad' --strict-json`
  - `CI=true pnpm docs:list`
  - `CI=true pnpm test src/cli/config-cli.test.ts`
  - `git diff --check`
- Evidence after fix (screenshot, recording, terminal capture, console output, redacted runtime log, linked artifact, or copied live output):
  - `openclaw config set gateway.port 19001 --strict-json` accepted numeric JSON input.
  - `openclaw config set channels.whatsapp.groups '["*"]' --strict-json` accepted array JSON input.
  - `openclaw config set gateway.port '{bad' --strict-json` printed `Could not parse "{bad" as JSON for --strict-json.` and `For plain strings, omit --strict-json.`
  - `CI=true pnpm docs:list` passed.
  - `CI=true pnpm test src/cli/config-cli.test.ts` passed with 76 tests.
  - `git diff --check` passed.
- Observed result after fix: the documentation now matches the observed strict parsing behavior and the CLI tests still pass.
- What was not tested: full repository test suite.
- Before evidence (optional but encouraged): issue #65345 described the docs/code drift around `--strict-json`.

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause: the docs described the normal JSON5 parsing fallback, but the strict parsing flag was not called out precisely enough.
- Missing detection / guardrail: docs review did not catch the distinction between normal value parsing and strict parsing.
- Contributing context (if known): `--json` remains as a legacy alias while `--strict-json` is the clearer name.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [ ] Unit test
  - [ ] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [x] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: `src/cli/config-cli.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in: strict JSON mode rejects invalid JSON instead of falling back to a plain string.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: the behavior already has targeted CLI coverage, this PR only updates docs.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): `src/cli/config-cli.test.ts`
- If no new test is added, why not: this is a docs-only clarification for behavior already covered by tests.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

Documentation only. CLI behavior is unchanged.

## Diagram (if applicable)

N/A

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: N/A

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Linux
- Runtime/container: Node v24.13.0 with pnpm
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel (if any): N/A
- Relevant config (redacted): temporary local config values only

### Steps

1. Review `docs/cli/config.md` strict JSON wording.
2. Run strict JSON CLI examples with valid number and array inputs.
3. Run strict JSON CLI example with invalid JSON input.
4. Run docs listing, targeted CLI tests, and whitespace diff check.

### Expected

- Docs describe strict JSON behavior accurately.
- Valid JSON values are accepted with `--strict-json`.
- Invalid JSON fails with the strict JSON error.
- Existing targeted tests pass.

### Actual

- Docs now describe strict JSON behavior accurately.
- Valid JSON values were accepted with `--strict-json`.
- Invalid JSON failed with `Could not parse "{bad" as JSON for --strict-json.`
- `CI=true pnpm docs:list`, `CI=true pnpm test src/cli/config-cli.test.ts`, and `git diff --check` passed.

## Evidence

target.title: Add browser dictation to Control UI chat
target.body:
## Summary
- Add a Web Speech API dictation button to the Control UI chat composer when SpeechRecognition is available
- Stream interim/final recognition text into the existing draft state
- Reset/stop dictation with chat view cleanup and cover supported/unsupported behavior in tests

## Real behavior proof
- **Behavior or issue addressed:** The Control UI chat composer exposes browser dictation/voice input when the Web Speech API is available, while keeping it hidden on unsupported browsers.
- **Real environment tested:** Real local OpenClaw installation on Linux, `OpenClaw 2026.5.7 (eeef486)`, Gateway reachable at `ws://127.0.0.1:18789`, systemd user Gateway service running.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** Ran a live terminal verification against the installed Control UI bundle served by the local OpenClaw setup:

  ```bash
  ROOT=/home/alqasem/.npm-global/lib/node_modules/openclaw/dist/control-ui
  openclaw --version
  openclaw status --json
  grep -o 'assets/index[^"[:space:]]*\\.js' "$ROOT/index.html" | tail -1
  ASSET=$(grep -o 'assets/index[^"[:space:]]*\\.js' "$ROOT/index.html" | tail -1)
  grep -Eo 'OC_STT_START|Voice input|Listening\\.\\.\\.|SpeechRecognition' "$ROOT/$ASSET" | sort -u
  systemctl --user is-active openclaw-control-ui-dictation-patch.path
  ```

- **Evidence after fix:** Terminal capture from the real local setup:

  ```text
  OpenClaw runtime: OpenClaw 2026.5.7 (eeef486)
  Gateway: reachable at ws://127.0.0.1:18789; service running (pid 126761, state active)
  Control UI script: assets/index-dictate-dfd3276f.js
  Dictation markers: Listening...,OC_STT_START SpeechRecognition,Voice input
  Hook: active
  ```

- **Observed result after fix:** The installed Control UI asset contains the dictation affordance strings and SpeechRecognition marker, confirming the real served Control UI includes browser dictation support. The local post-update hook is active to keep the installed UI patched until this upstream change lands.
- **What was not tested:** Manual microphone permission / live speech recognition in the browser was not re-tested during this PR update; the local runtime proof verifies the real Control UI payload contains and serves the dictation behavior markers, while automated tests cover supported/unsupported SpeechRecognition behavior and transcript insertion.

## Test plan
- `corepack pnpm --dir ui exec vitest run --config vitest.config.ts src/ui/views/chat.test.ts`
- `corepack pnpm tsgo:test:ui`
- `corepack pnpm --dir ui build`

Note: a broader UI test run was blocked by a missing local Playwright browser install, but the targeted chat test suite passed.

## Scope (select all touched areas)
- [x] Core runtime / infra
- [x] Tests

## Linked Issue/PR
Closes #66489

## Real behavior proof
Behavior or issue addressed: Delivery queue recovery now treats deterministic outbound delivery failures for disallowed local media paths and overlong messages as permanent errors instead of retrying failures that cannot succeed.

Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw source checkout on macOS, using the repository pnpm workspace and Node from `/opt/homebrew/bin`.

Exact steps or command run after this patch: Ran the production classifier from the local OpenClaw checkout with a real Node/tsx command against the patched source:

```bash
PATH=/opt/homebrew/bin:$PATH pnpm exec tsx -e "import { isPermanentDeliveryError } from './src/infra/outbound/delivery-queue.ts'; const cases = ['Local media path is not under an allowed directory: ./some-local-file.png', 'Bad Request: message is too long', 'network down']; for (const msg of cases) console.log(JSON.stringify({ msg, permanent: isPermanentDeliveryError(msg) }));"
```

Evidence after fix: Terminal output copied from the local OpenClaw checkout after the patch:

```text
{"msg":"Local media path is not under an allowed directory: ./some-local-file.png","permanent":true}
{"msg":"Bad Request: message is too long","permanent":true}
{"msg":"network down","permanent":false}
```

Observed result after fix: The two deterministic delivery failures are classified as permanent, while a transient `network down` error remains retryable.

What was not tested: No additional gaps

## Root Cause (if applicable)
`PERMANENT_ERROR_PATTERNS` did not include the deterministic sandbox path rejection or overlong message rejection, so those failures were treated like retryable transient delivery errors.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)
RED before the implementation:

```bash
PATH=/opt/homebrew/bin:$PATH pnpm test src/infra/outbound/delivery-queue.policy.test.ts
```

Result before the classifier change:

```text
2 failed | 22 passed (24)
- Local media path is not under an allowed directory: ./some-local-file.png
- Bad Request: message is too long
```

GREEN after the implementation:


```text
24 passed (24)
```


Result:

```text
exit code 0
```

```bash
git diff --check
```

Result:

```text
exit code 0
```

Known broader-suite note:

```bash
PATH=/opt/homebrew/bin:$PATH pnpm test:changed
```

This ran a broad changed-test lane. The touched outbound policy test passed, but the command failed on unrelated/baseline tests outside this PR's diff:

- `src/auto-reply/reply/completion-delivery-policy.test.ts` failed 4 legacy session-key inference assertions.
- I confirmed those same 4 failures reproduce on clean `main` with the PR changes stashed.
- `src/plugin-activation-boundary.test.ts` failed once during the broad run but passed when rerun directly on this branch.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes
Delivery queue recovery now treats these deterministic failures as permanent instead of spending retry budget on attempts that cannot succeed:

- `Local media path is not under an allowed directory: ...`
- `message is too long`

## Security Impact (required)
No new permissions, network access, secrets, or data exposure. This only extends local error classification patterns used by the existing delivery queue retry policy.

## Repro + Verification
### Environment
- macOS local source checkout
- Node via `/opt/homebrew/bin`
- pnpm workspace from the repository lockfile

### Steps
1. Add regression cases for the two deterministic error strings.
2. Confirm they fail before updating `PERMANENT_ERROR_PATTERNS`.
3. Add the two permanent-error regexes.
4. Rerun targeted tests and changed checks.

### Expected
Both deterministic delivery failures classify as permanent errors; transient errors such as `network down` remain retryable.

### Actual
Matches expected.

## Evidence
- `pnpm test src/infra/outbound/delivery-queue.policy.test.ts` → 24 passed.
- `pnpm check:changed` → exit code 0.
- `git diff --check` → exit code 0.
- Direct production helper import output shown above.

## Human Verification (required)
Local verification was run in the source checkout with the exact commands and outputs listed above.

## Review Conversations
I will respond to review comments in-thread and keep follow-up changes scoped to this issue.

## Compatibility / Migration
No config or storage migration required. Existing failed queue entries are not rewritten; future recovery attempts will classify matching errors as permanent.

## Risks and Mitigations
- Risk: a provider could use `message is too long` for a condition that is not permanent. Mitigation: this phrase represents a deterministic payload-size rejection in the delivery path, and the test keeps unrelated transient messages retryable.
- Risk: string matching can be broad. Mitigation: this PR only adds the two issue-reported phrases to the existing explicit permanent-error classifier.

target.title: feat(voice-call): implement asynchronous email delivery for consult results
target.body:
## Summary

- **Problem:** During realtime voice calls, complex questions (e.g., data queries requiring tool execution) block the caller waiting for an answer that may take minutes, with no fallback if the consult times out or the caller prefers email delivery.
- **Why it matters:** Callers hang up or get silence during long-running agent consults. There's no mechanism to acknowledge the request and deliver results asynchronously.
- **What changed:** Added background email delivery for voice-call consults with two paths: (1) **email-first** — caller explicitly requests email delivery, agent acks immediately and researches in background; (2) **timeout** — if consult exceeds `backgroundConsultTimeoutMs`, ack the caller and email the result when ready. New config fields: `backgroundEmailDeliveryEnabled` (explicit toggle, default `false`), `backgroundConsultTimeoutMs`, `backgroundEmailPrompt`, `recipientEmail`. New file `background-email-delivery.ts` handles fire-and-forget email delivery via embedded agent runs. Added `deliveryPreference` arg to the consult tool schema. Improved system prompts to prevent narration in background runs and prevent re-querying in email delivery. Email delivery agent spawns are restricted to `["exec", "memory_search", "memory_get"]` tools only. All logs redacted to structural IDs (no args/transcript/result text). Timeout timer properly cleaned up after `Promise.race`.
- **What did NOT change (scope boundary):** Normal voice consult path is untouched. No changes to the realtime voice provider, webhook server, call manager, or telephony providers. Core `consultRealtimeVoiceAgent` only gained an optional `abortSignal` param — no behavioral change. Plugin SDK barrel export additions only.

## Change Type (select all)

- [ ] Bug fix
- [x] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [x] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [x] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Related # (none — feature request from internal testing)
- [ ] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- **Behavior or issue addressed:** Voice call consults that exceed timeout now ack the caller and deliver results by email. Callers can also explicitly request email delivery. Feature is off by default and requires explicit opt-in via `backgroundEmailDeliveryEnabled: true`.
- **Real environment tested:** Local OpenClaw dev with Twilio realtime voice, Claude (Anthropic) provider
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** Configured `backgroundEmailDeliveryEnabled: true`, `backgroundConsultTimeoutMs: 15000` and `recipientEmail`, called the voice number, asked a complex data query, observed timeout path trigger and email delivery spawn
- **Evidence after fix:** Logs show `Timeout path: 15000ms exceeded`, `Email-first path: returning ack`, `Spawning email delivery agent`, `Background email delivery completed`
- **Observed result after fix:** Caller receives spoken ack ("I'll research that and send you an email"), background consult completes, email delivery agent spawns and sends email
- **What was not tested:** Tests not yet updated after iterative changes; `pnpm check:changed` not yet run
- **Before evidence:** Before this change, long consults blocked the caller indefinitely or timed out with an error

## Root Cause (if applicable)

N/A — new feature

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should catch this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [x] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- **Target test or file:** `extensions/voice-call/src/runtime.background-email.test.ts`, `extensions/voice-call/src/background-email-delivery.test.ts`, `src/realtime-voice/agent-consult-tool.test.ts`
- **Scenario the test should lock in:** email-first path returns ack and spawns delivery; timeout path races and falls back to email; `parseRealtimeVoiceAgentConsultArgs` normalizes `deliveryPreference`; `buildRealtimeVoiceAgentConsultEmailAckResponse` returns correct ack text; both paths gated by `backgroundEmailDeliveryEnabled`; disabled toggle falls through to normal consult
- **Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail:** Unit tests on the tool schema/args + integration test on the runtime handler paths cover all branching logic
- **If no new test is added, why not:** Test files exist but need updating after iterative changes

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- New config fields under `plugins.entries.voice-call.config.realtime`:
  - `backgroundEmailDeliveryEnabled` (boolean, default `false`) — master toggle for the entire background email delivery feature; must be `true` to enable email-first and timeout paths
  - `backgroundConsultTimeoutMs` (number, optional) — timeout before falling back to email (requires `backgroundEmailDeliveryEnabled`)
  - `backgroundEmailPrompt` (string, optional) — custom system prompt for email delivery agent
  - `recipientEmail` (string, email, optional) — pre-configured recipient; voice agent won't ask for email
- New `deliveryPreference` argument in the realtime voice consult tool (`"voice"` default, `"email"` for explicit email request) — only honored when `backgroundEmailDeliveryEnabled` is `true`
- Voice agent instructions updated to mention email delivery option
- Graceful shutdown now flushes pending background deliveries

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
[caller asks question] -> [consult agent] -> [wait...] -> [timeout/error OR answer]

After (email-first, backgroundEmailDeliveryEnabled: true):
[caller asks "email me the answer"] -> [ack immediately] -> [background consult] -> [email delivery agent] -> [email sent]

After (timeout, backgroundEmailDeliveryEnabled: true):
[caller asks question] -> [race: consult vs timeout]
  -> consult wins: [return answer to caller]
  -> timeout wins: [ack caller] -> [consult continues] -> [email delivery agent] -> [email sent]

After (backgroundEmailDeliveryEnabled: false or unset):
[caller asks question] -> [consult agent] -> [return answer to caller]  (same as before)
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? `No`
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? `No`
- New/changed network calls? `No` — email delivery uses existing exec tool (himalaya/gog CLI)
- Command/tool execution surface changed? `Restricted` — email delivery agent spawns are limited to `["exec", "memory_search", "memory_get"]` tools via `toolsAllow`
- Data access scope changed? `No`
- PII in logs? `No` — all tool handler and email delivery logs redacted to structural IDs only (no args, transcript, or result text)

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS
- Runtime/container: Node 22+
- Model/provider: Anthropic Claude (sonnet)
- Integration/channel: Twilio voice-call realtime
- Relevant config (redacted): `realtime.enabled: true`, `realtime.backgroundEmailDeliveryEnabled: true`, `realtime.backgroundConsultTimeoutMs: 15000`, `realtime.recipientEmail: <redacted>`

### Steps

1. Configure voice-call plugin with `backgroundEmailDeliveryEnabled: true`, `backgroundConsultTimeoutMs`, and `recipientEmail`
2. Call the configured phone number
3. Ask a complex question that requires tool execution (e.g., "What are January purchases by location?")
4. Observe: caller gets ack after timeout, email arrives with full answer

### Expected

- Caller receives spoken acknowledgment after timeout
- Background consult completes with full data
- Email delivery agent sends the answer

### Actual

- Confirmed via live testing with Twilio + Anthropic

## Evidence

- [x] Trace/log snippets (runtime logs showing all three paths)
- [ ] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)
```text
14:34:56 [voice-call] realtime tool call executing callId=4b560e0d-15b9-444f-85fc-2f09090c59fd tool=openclaw_agent_consult hasHandler=true
14:34:56 [plugins] [voice-call] Tool handler invoked: tool=openclaw_agent_consult, call=4b560e0d-15b9-444f-85fc-2f09090c59fd, args={"question":"***","context":"User asked: \"Can you please give me a ***\"","responseStyle":"warm, friendly, professional","deliveryPreference":"voice"}, partialTranscript=null
14:34:56 [plugins] [voice-call] Timeout path: racing consult against 15000ms for call=4b560e0d-15b9-444f-85fc-2f09090c59fd, agent=main, session=voice:573042634248
14:35:11 [plugins] [voice-call] Timeout path: 15000ms exceeded for call=4b560e0d-15b9-444f-85fc-2f09090c59fd, deferring to email delivery
14:35:11 [voice-call] realtime tool call completed callId=4b560e0d-15b9-444f-85fc-2f09090c59fd tool=openclaw_agent_consult status=ok elapsedMs=15009
14:35:43 [plugins] [voice-call] Timeout path: background consult resolved for call=4b560e0d-15b9-444f-85fc-2f09090c59fd, result="You have ***"
14:35:43 [plugins] [voice-call] Spawning email delivery agent for session=voice:573042634248, agent=main, question="Provide a summary of ***", consultResult="You have ***"
```

## Human Verification (required)

- **Verified scenarios:** email-first path, timeout path, normal path (unaffected), graceful shutdown flush, feature disabled by default (no behavior change without opt-in)
- **Edge cases checked:** missing recipientEmail (falls back to memory lookup), no backgroundConsultTimeoutMs (skips timeout path), empty consult result (fallbackText), `backgroundEmailDeliveryEnabled: false` (email-first and timeout paths skipped, normal consult only), `deliveryPreference: "email"` ignored when feature disabled
- **What you did not verify:** `pnpm check:changed`, test suite after iterative changes, email actually received (email CLI tool availability on test machine)

## Review Conversations

- [ ] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [ ] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? `Yes` — all new config fields are optional; `backgroundEmailDeliveryEnabled` defaults to `false`, so no behavior change without explicit opt-in
- Config/env changes? `Yes` — four new optional config fields under `realtime`
- Migration needed? `No`

## Risks and Mitigations

- **Risk:** Background consult promise leaks if process crashes before email delivery
  - **Mitigation:** `pendingBackgroundDeliveries` tracked and flushed on graceful shutdown; `.finally()` cleanup on each promise
- **Risk:** Email delivery agent re-queries data instead of using provided answer
  - **Mitigation:** System prompt explicitly forbids re-querying; answer delimited with clear markers; prompt says "Do NOT call any data or API tools"
- **Risk:** Email delivery agent spawns with excessive tool access
  - **Mitigation:** `toolsAllow` restricted to `["exec", "memory_search", "memory_get"]` only
- **Risk:** Timeout timer leaks if consult resolves first
  - **Mitigation:** `clearTimeout(timeoutHandle)` called after `Promise.race` resolves

target.title: feat(cli): add openclaw context map command
target.body:
## Summary

Adds `openclaw context map` — a CLI command that renders the same WinDirStat-style context treemap PNG that `/context map` sends in-chat, but from the terminal without needing a connected channel.

```
openclaw context map                           # most recently active session
openclaw context map --session agent:main:me   # specific session key
openclaw context map --output ~/ctx.png        # save to a specific path
```

The command calls `sessions.list` with `includeContextWeight: true` via the gateway RPC, picks the most recently updated session that has a cached run report, and feeds the `SessionSystemPromptReport` into the existing `renderContextTreemapPng` renderer. If no run report exists yet (no messages sent in the session), it prints a clear hint to send a message first.

### Files changed

- `src/cli/context-cli.ts` — barrel export
- `src/cli/context-cli/register.ts` — command implementation
- `src/cli/program/register.subclis-core.ts` — wired into the CLI loader
- `src/cli/program/subcli-descriptors.ts` — registered in the help index
- `src/cli/command-catalog.ts` — policy entry (`loadPlugins: "never"`, `networkProxy: "bypass"`)

## Verification

Build passes clean:
```
pnpm build  # exit 0
```

Type check passes clean:
```
pnpm tsgo --noEmit  # 0 errors
```

Help output:
```
$ openclaw context --help
Usage: openclaw context [options] [command]

Inspect session context usage

Commands:
  map  Render a treemap PNG of the current session context contributors

$ openclaw context map --help
Usage: openclaw context map [options]

Render a treemap PNG of the current session context contributors

Options:
  --session <key>   Session key to inspect (defaults to most recently active)
  --output <path>   Output path for the PNG (defaults to openclaw tmp dir)
  --url <url>       Gateway WebSocket URL
  --token <token>   Gateway token (if required)
  --timeout <ms>    Timeout in ms (default: "30000")
  --expect-final    Wait for final response (agent) (default: false)
```

target.title: Fix Discord gzip response parsing
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: Discord `message read` and `search` could receive raw gzip bytes and surface compressed binary instead of parsed JSON.
- Why it matters: callers expect parsed Discord API data; compressed bytes break message read/search workflows before normalization.
- What changed: the Discord REST client now defensively decodes raw gzip response bodies, and message read/search add response-shape guards with clear errors.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): no Discord endpoint behavior, auth, request params, or network call shape changed.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #80764
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: raw gzipped Discord REST response bodies now decode into JSON before `message read` / `search` callers see them.
- Real environment tested: local OpenClaw checkout running the real Discord `RequestClient`, `readMessagesDiscord`, and `searchMessagesDiscord` code paths against a local HTTP endpoint that returns raw gzipped response bytes.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: `node --import tsx` with a local HTTP endpoint serving gzipped JSON bytes to the real Discord REST client.
- Evidence after fix: copied terminal output from the runtime proof:

```text
[proof-server] GET /api/v10/channels/proof-channel/messages?limit=1 -> raw gzip bytes=77
[proof-server] GET /api/v10/guilds/proof-guild/messages/search?content=decoded&limit=1 -> raw gzip bytes=101
{
  "readContent": "decoded read gzip",
  "searchContent": "decoded search gzip"
}
```

- Observed result after fix: both read and search returned decoded JSON content from raw gzip bytes.
- What was not tested: live Discord API credentials/account flow.
- Before evidence: the same raw-gzip response shape is the issue shape that previously surfaced compressed binary instead of parsed JSON.

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause: the Discord REST client relied on `response.text()`, but some runtimes can expose the raw gzipped body bytes instead of decoded text.
- Missing detection / guardrail: there was no coverage for raw gzipped Discord JSON response bodies, and read/search did not validate the response shape before returning data.
- Contributing context (if known): unknown.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [ ] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: `extensions/discord/src/internal/rest.test.ts`, `extensions/discord/src/send.messages.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in: raw gzip-compressed JSON parses correctly; already-decoded text remains unchanged; read/search reject invalid response shapes clearly.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: the bug is in REST response decoding and the immediate message API contracts.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): none for gzipped raw bodies.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

Discord message read/search responses that arrive gzip-compressed now return parsed JSON instead of compressed binary text. Invalid response shapes now report a clear error.

## Diagram (if applicable)

N/A

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: N/A

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS
- Runtime/container: local repo via pnpm / Node
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel (if any): Discord extension
- Relevant config (redacted): none

### Steps

1. Serve gzipped JSON bytes from a local HTTP endpoint without relying on Discord credentials.
2. Point the real Discord `RequestClient` at that endpoint.
3. Call `readMessagesDiscord` and `searchMessagesDiscord` through the normal Discord helper functions.

### Expected

- Response bodies are parsed as JSON and invalid response shapes fail clearly.

### Actual

- Matches expected after this patch.

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [x] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

Focused verification passed:

```text
pnpm vitest run extensions/discord/src/internal/rest.test.ts extensions/discord/src/actions/runtime.test.ts extensions/discord/src/send.messages.test.ts -- --reporter=verbose
Test Files  3 passed
Tests       76 passed
```

Additional checks passed:

```text
git diff --check
pnpm exec oxfmt --check extensions/discord/src/internal/rest.ts extensions/discord/src/internal/rest.test.ts extensions/discord/src/send.messages.ts extensions/discord/src/send.messages.test.ts extensions/discord/src/actions/runtime.messaging.messages.ts
CI=1 pnpm check:changed
```

## Human Verification (required)

- Verified scenarios: raw gzip decode path, already-decoded body path, read response array guard, search response object guard, changed-file type/lint checks.
- Edge cases checked: empty body, already-decoded JSON text, invalid read/search response shapes.
- What you did **not** verify: live Discord API account flow.

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Config/env changes? (`Yes/No`) No
- Migration needed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If yes, exact upgrade steps: N/A

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: manually decompressing could double-decompress in fetch implementations that already decode gzip.
  - Mitigation: decode only when the raw body starts with gzip magic bytes and add a regression test for already-decoded bodies.

target.title: fix(tools): let apply_patch inherit trusted exec mode
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: `apply_patch` stayed workspace-contained even when `exec` was explicitly configured as `security=full` and `ask=off`, forcing trusted owner/operator setups to manage a second, protected `tools.exec.applyPatch.workspaceOnly=false` knob.
- Why it matters: no-approval full exec can already write anywhere the selected host permits, so making `apply_patch` stricter in that mode is inconsistent friction rather than a meaningful security boundary.
- What changed: when `tools.exec.applyPatch.workspaceOnly` is unset, `apply_patch` now inherits configured no-approval full-exec host-write authority; explicit `applyPatch.workspaceOnly=true` and `tools.fs.workspaceOnly=true` still force workspace containment.
- What did NOT change: `tools.exec.applyPatch.workspaceOnly=false` remains an explicit dangerous config flag, and approval/allowlist exec modes still keep `apply_patch` workspace-contained by default.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [x] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [x] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Related #77886
- Related #62006
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: `apply_patch` outside the workspace should work when the operator has already configured trusted no-approval full exec (`tools.exec.security=full`, `tools.exec.ask=off`) and has not explicitly forced `applyPatch.workspaceOnly=true` or `tools.fs.workspaceOnly=true`.
- Real environment tested: local OpenClaw source checkout on Linux, Node 24, branch `seb/apply-patch-inherits-full-exec`.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:
  ```sh
  PATH="/tmp/openclaw-corepack-bin:/home/linuxbrew/.linuxbrew/Cellar/node@24/24.15.0/bin:$PATH" pnpm exec tsx - <<'TS'
  import fs from 'node:fs/promises';
  import os from 'node:os';
  import path from 'node:path';
  import { createOpenClawCodingTools } from './src/agents/pi-tools.ts';

  const workspace = await fs.mkdtemp(path.join(os.tmpdir(), 'openclaw-real-applypatch-'));
  const escaped = path.join(path.dirname(workspace), `openclaw-real-applypatch-${process.pid}.txt`);
  const rel = path.relative(workspace, escaped);
  try {
    const tools = createOpenClawCodingTools({
      workspaceDir: workspace,
      agentDir: workspace,
      config: { tools: { allow: ['read', 'write', 'exec'], exec: { security: 'full', ask: 'off' } } },
      modelProvider: 'openai',
      modelId: 'gpt-5.5',
    });
    const tool = tools.find((t) => t.name === 'apply_patch');
    if (!tool) throw new Error('apply_patch missing');
    await (tool as any).execute('manual-check', {
      input: `*** Begin Patch\n*** Add File: ${rel}\n+ok\n*** End Patch`,
    });
    const content = await fs.readFile(escaped, 'utf8');
    console.log(JSON.stringify({ workspace, escapedExists: content === 'ok\n', content }));
  } finally {
    await fs.rm(escaped, { force: true });
    await fs.rm(workspace, { recursive: true, force: true });
  }
  TS
  ```
- Evidence after fix:
  ```json
  {"workspace":"/tmp/openclaw-real-applypatch-WfVLht","escapedExists":true,"content":"ok\n"}
  ```
- Observed result after fix: the real `apply_patch` tool instance wrote the file outside the temporary workspace under configured `security=full` + `ask=off`.
- What was not tested: a full live Gateway restart/session using this source checkout; the live installed Gateway was not modified.
- Before evidence (optional but encouraged): existing regression coverage documents the old behavior in `pi-tools-agent-config.test.ts`: absent an explicit `workspaceOnly=false`, outside-workspace `apply_patch` rejected with `Path escapes sandbox root`.

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause: `createOpenClawCodingTools()` computed `applyPatchWorkspaceOnly` solely from `tools.fs.workspaceOnly` and `tools.exec.applyPatch.workspaceOnly`, ignoring the effective configured exec trust mode.
- Missing detection / guardrail: tests covered explicit `applyPatch.workspaceOnly=false`, but not the coherent trusted-exec case (`security=full`, `ask=off`).
- Contributing context (if known): `tools.exec.applyPatch.workspaceOnly=false` is correctly treated as dangerous/protected for agent-initiated config mutation, which made the duplicate knob especially awkward for owner-operated trusted deployments.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [ ] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: `src/agents/pi-tools-agent-config.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in: full exec with approvals off allows outside-workspace `apply_patch` when `applyPatch.workspaceOnly` is unset; full exec with approvals on still blocks; explicit `applyPatch.workspaceOnly=true` and `tools.fs.workspaceOnly=true` still block.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: it exercises the actual tool construction path and `apply_patch` execution without requiring a Gateway process.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): none for trusted exec inheritance; existing explicit opt-out test remains.
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- In configured `tools.exec.security=full` + `tools.exec.ask=off` mode, `apply_patch` can write outside the workspace without also setting `tools.exec.applyPatch.workspaceOnly=false`.
- Operators can still force workspace containment with `tools.exec.applyPatch.workspaceOnly=true` or the umbrella `tools.fs.workspaceOnly=true`.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
exec full/off -> shell can write outside workspace
exec full/off -> apply_patch still blocked unless applyPatch.workspaceOnly=false

After:
exec full/off + applyPatch.workspaceOnly unset -> apply_patch inherits host-write authority
exec full/off + applyPatch.workspaceOnly=true or tools.fs.workspaceOnly=true -> still workspace-contained
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: This intentionally makes `apply_patch` match an already trusted/no-approval full-exec configuration. In that mode, `exec` can already write arbitrary files using shell commands. Mitigations: approval/allowlist exec modes still keep `apply_patch` workspace-contained by default; explicit `tools.exec.applyPatch.workspaceOnly=true` and `tools.fs.workspaceOnly=true` override inheritance and force workspace containment.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Linux
- Runtime/container: direct local source checkout, Node 24
- Model/provider: OpenAI provider/tool registration path (`modelProvider: openai`, `modelId: gpt-5.5`)
- Integration/channel (if any): N/A
- Relevant config (redacted): `{ "tools": { "allow": ["read", "write", "exec"], "exec": { "security": "full", "ask": "off" } } }`

### Steps

1. Create a temporary workspace.
2. Construct OpenClaw coding tools with `tools.exec.security=full` and `tools.exec.ask=off`.
3. Invoke `apply_patch` to add a file outside the workspace via a relative `..` path.

### Expected

- `apply_patch` writes the file because no-approval full exec already grants equivalent host-write authority.

### Actual

- The file was created outside the workspace and read back as `ok\n`.

## Evidence

Attach at least one:

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [x] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

Targeted checks run:

```sh
pnpm exec vitest run src/agents/pi-tools-agent-config.test.ts src/agents/apply-patch.test.ts src/agents/openclaw-gateway-tool.test.ts src/security/dangerous-config-flags.test.ts
# Test Files 7 passed (7); Tests 140 passed (140)

pnpm exec oxfmt --check src/agents/pi-tools.ts src/agents/pi-tools-agent-config.test.ts src/config/types.tools.ts src/config/schema.help.ts docs/tools/apply-patch.md docs/tools/exec.md
# All matched files use the correct format.

pnpm check:changed
# exited 0
```

## Human Verification (required)

What you personally verified (not just CI), and how:

- Verified scenarios:
  - trusted `security=full` + `ask=off` inherits host-write authority for `apply_patch` when unset.
  - `security=full` + `ask=on-miss` remains workspace-contained by default.
  - `tools.fs.workspaceOnly=true` still forces workspace containment.
  - explicit `tools.exec.applyPatch.workspaceOnly=true` still forces workspace containment.
- Edge cases checked: existing explicit `tools.exec.applyPatch.workspaceOnly=false` behavior still works; gateway protected-config tests still pass unchanged.
- What you did **not** verify: live installed Gateway behavior after replacing the installed OpenClaw package with this branch.

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes/No`) Yes, for approval/allowlist/default workspace-contained modes; intentional behavior change for configured no-approval full exec.
- Config/env changes? (`Yes/No`) No
- Migration needed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If yes, exact upgrade steps: N/A

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: deployments with `security=full` + `ask=off` that expected `apply_patch` to remain workspace-only when unset will see broader `apply_patch` writes.
  - Mitigation: set `tools.exec.applyPatch.workspaceOnly=true` or `tools.fs.workspaceOnly=true`; docs and schema help now call this out.

target.title: docs: add Codex long-task reliability runbook
target.body:
## Summary

Adds a public Codex long-task reliability runbook based on a field deployment where long Telegram/cron tasks kept appearing stalled or failing even after queue/concurrency tuning.

The report is intentionally sanitized and generic. It documents the key distinction we kept missing as operators:

- `messages.queue.mode=collect` helps direct-chat follow-ups avoid interrupting active turns.
- `cron.maxConcurrentRuns` and `agents.defaults.maxConcurrent` control worker capacity.
- `appServer.requestTimeoutMs` controls Codex app-server control-plane requests.
- `appServer.turnCompletionIdleTimeoutMs` controls the quiet window after turn-scoped app-server requests while OpenClaw waits for `turn/completed`.

The last one is what made long jobs still fail after the other knobs were tuned.

## Field Evidence

Observed on a local macOS LaunchAgent deployment running stable `2026.5.7`:

- `messages.queue.mode: collect`
- `agents.defaults.maxConcurrent: 4`
- `cron.maxConcurrentRuns: 3`
- `plugins.entries.codex.config.appServer.requestTimeoutMs: 1200000`
- repeated logs like:
  - `codex app-server turn idle timed out waiting for completion`
  - `embedded run failover decision ... reason=timeout ... rawError=codex app-server attempt timed out`
  - occasional `timeout-compaction` when direct-session context was high

Operator mitigations tried before this doc:

- raised `appServer.requestTimeoutMs`
- kept Telegram queueing in `collect`
- increased local concurrency
- moved explicit long work to isolated cron jobs
- added checkpoint files and progress conventions
- reset high-context direct Telegram sessions

Those helped queueing and recovery but did not fully address the Codex turn-completion quiet-window failure mode.

## What This PR Changes

- Adds `docs/plugins/codex-harness-long-tasks.md` with:
  - quick fix using `turnCompletionIdleTimeoutMs`
  - diagnosis commands
  - symptom table
  - queueing vs durability explanation
  - checkpointing pattern for long background work
  - maintainer follow-up ideas
- Links the new page from Codex harness troubleshooting and the Codex harness reference timeout section.
- Adds the page to Mintlify navigation.

## Validation

- `git diff --check`
- `PATH="/usr/local/lib/node_modules/corepack/shims:$PATH" pnpm check:docs`

target.title: fix: add @zed-industries/codex-acp to minimumReleaseAgeExclude
target.body:
## Problem

`@zed-industries/codex-acp` and its platform-specific packages (`@zed-industries/codex-acp-darwin-arm64`, `-x64`, `-linux-arm64`, etc.) are transitive dependencies that release frequently. The `minimumReleaseAge: 2880` (48-hour) guard in `pnpm-workspace.yaml` blocks `pnpm install` whenever a new version ships less than 48 hours before a scheduled install run, causing the install to fail.

## Fix

Add `@zed-industries/codex-acp` and `@zed-industries/codex-acp-*` to `minimumReleaseAgeExclude`, matching the pattern already used for `@openai/codex` and `@openai/codex-*`.

## Why this pattern is safe

These are native binary packages (Zed ACP runtime). They follow the same release cadence pattern as `@openai/codex-*` which is already excluded. Blocking on release age provides no meaningful safety benefit for first-party tooling packages from known publishers.

target.title: feat(telegram): presence indicator (typing + phased reactions)
target.body:
## Summary
- Adds three-phase presence indicator for Telegram messages: 👀 on receive, 👨‍💻 + typing while processing, cleared on done.
- Eliminates silent-agent UX where users can't tell if a message was received or is still being worked on.
- Per-account config under `channels.telegram[.accounts[*]].presence` (`typing`, `reaction`, `workingReaction`); defaults on for new accounts, backward-compatible.

## Implementation
- New `extensions/telegram/src/presence-indicator.ts` exporting `PresenceIndicator` / `PresenceCtx` and a Telegram-specific factory built on the existing Bot API client and the 401-backoff-safe ``sendChatAction`` handler.
- Hook points:
  - ``onMessageReceived`` fires inside ``bot.on('message')`` and ``bot.on('channel_post')`` in ``bot-handlers.runtime.ts``, before queueing/dispatch.
  - ``onProcessingStart`` / ``onProcessingEnd`` wrap ``dispatchTelegramMessage`` in ``bot-message.ts`` using try/finally so the reaction is always cleared.
- Edge cases handled per spec: 400ms debounce on typing, ref-counted per (chatId, threadId) so concurrent processors share one loop, warn-and-swallow on Telegram API failures, and a 60s TTL watchdog that clears orphan reactions if ``onProcessingEnd`` never fires.

## Test plan
- [x] Unit (``presence-indicator.test.ts``): lifecycle order, typing-loop interval, <400ms debounce, reaction state, failure tolerance, ref-counted concurrency, orphan watchdog.
- [x] Config: ``presence.typing: false`` disables the loop; ``presence.reaction: null`` disables reactions entirely; custom emoji overrides respected.
- [x] Processor (``bot-message.test.ts``): ``onProcessingStart`` runs before dispatch and ``onProcessingEnd`` after, including the dispatch-throws path.
- [ ] Manual: send a message via a test bot, verify 👀 → 👨‍💻 → cleared with "typing..." visible.

## Notes
- ``setMessageReaction`` was already wired in the channel; this PR adds an additional explicit lifecycle on top, gated by config so existing ``ackReaction`` users are unaffected when ``presence`` is absent.
- Pre-existing unrelated test failures in ``setup-surface.test.ts``, ``bot.create-telegram-bot.test.ts (pairing)``, ``channel.message-adapter.test.ts``, and ``extensions/whatsapp/src/login.coverage.test.ts`` reproduce on clean ``main`` in this environment (profile-name string mismatches, vitest mock-type drift). Not introduced here.

Reported by @kotaro.

target.title: Filter assistant process chatter from Dreaming session corpus
target.body:
Fixes #80664. Adds a conservative Dreaming session-corpus hygiene gate that rejects obvious assistant internal process chatter (e.g. `Assistant: Need commit PR`, `Assistant: Now inspect`, `Assistant: Oops ... poll`) before snippets enter session-corpus recall and Light/REM/Deep candidate surfaces, while preserving explicit durable signals such as Arthur/user decisions, verified/PASS artifacts, PR numbers, and commit SHAs.

Validation: added focused tests in `dreaming-phases.test.ts`. Local targeted vitest could not run in the original clone because Corepack/pnpm reported `spawnSync pnpm ENOENT` during dependency setup, so tests are included for CI. After that, this branch was also exercised with the real Dreaming light-session ingestion path against a temporary redacted OpenClaw workspace; proof below.

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior or issue addressed: Dreaming session-corpus ingestion should reject obvious assistant internal process chatter before it can enter session-corpus recall / Light / REM / Deep candidate surfaces, while preserving durable decision and verification signals.
- Real environment tested: OpenClaw branch `memory-dreaming-session-corpus-filter` at commit `55b333c6b604df07016a050cb67d3342f8d961ef`, run on a Linux OpenClaw workspace clone with Node `v22.22.0`. The run created a temporary real OpenClaw workspace under `/tmp`, wrote a redacted session transcript under that workspace's `OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR`, then invoked the branch's Dreaming light-session ingestion hook.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:

```bash
cd /root/.openclaw/workspace/tmp/pr80666-proof/openclaw
git checkout pr-80666
git rev-parse HEAD  # 55b333c6b604df07016a050cb67d3342f8d961ef
node --import tsx tmp/pr80666-real-proof.ts | tee /root/.openclaw/workspace/tmp/pr80666-proof/proof-output.txt
```

- Evidence after fix (terminal capture / copied live output, paths redacted):

```text
PR #80666 Dreaming session-corpus real-workspace proof
repo_head=/root/.openclaw/workspace/tmp/pr80666-proof/openclaw
workspace=/tmp/openclaw-pr80666-real-workspace-REDACTED
state_dir=/tmp/openclaw-pr80666-real-workspace-REDACTED/.state
session_transcript=/tmp/openclaw-pr80666-real-workspace-REDACTED/.state/agents/main/sessions/real-pr80666-redacted-session.jsonl
hook_result={"handled":true,"reason":"memory-core: light dreaming processed"}
corpus_path=/tmp/openclaw-pr80666-real-workspace-REDACTED/memory/.dreams/session-corpus/2026-05-12.txt
rejected:PASS_ABSENT:Assistant: Need commit PR.
rejected:PASS_ABSENT:Assistant: Now inspect.
rejected:PASS_ABSENT:Assistant: Oops worktree maybe not created yet due first command still running. poll.
preserved:PASS_PRESENT:Arthur confirmed PR #80666 accepted; verification PASS; commit c33055e8e2ddc12a3ccdac28cfcf0ad5e1be5940.
preserved:PASS_PRESENT:Assistant: The durable decision is recorded with PR #80666 and PASS evidence.
--- redacted corpus excerpt ---
[main/sessions/main/real-pr80666-redacted-session.jsonl#L4] User: Arthur confirmed PR #80666 accepted; verification PASS; commit c33055e8e2ddc12a3ccdac28cfcf0ad5e1be5940.
[main/sessions/main/real-pr80666-redacted-session.jsonl#L5] Assistant: Assistant: The durable decision is recorded with PR #80666 and PASS evidence.
--- end excerpt ---
```

- Observed result after fix: The generated `memory/.dreams/session-corpus/2026-05-12.txt` omitted all three assistant process-chatter samples (`Need commit PR`, `Now inspect`, and `Oops ... poll`) and retained the durable `Arthur confirmed PR #80666 ... PASS ... commit c33055e8...` signal plus an assistant-authored durable PR/PASS line. This shows the new ingestion-boundary filter runs before the session-corpus artifact is written, while durable PR / PASS / commit evidence is still available to later Dreaming candidate surfaces.
- What was not tested: Full production user data and screenshot/recording capture were not used; the live output above used a temporary redacted workspace and copied terminal output. Full CI remains supplemental.

target.title: docs: clarify backend client behavior with gateway.auth.mode 'none'
target.body:
## Summary

This PR clarifies an important behavior of `gateway.auth.mode: "none"` that is not currently documented.

## Problem

When `gateway.auth.mode` is set to `"none"`, the gateway does not generate or provide a shared authentication token. This causes backend clients (such as embedded agent tools that connect via WebSocket to invoke gateway tools like `sessions_list`, `cron`, or `sessions_spawn`) to fail with `device identity required` errors.

## Evidence

From Gateway logs:

```
# Gateway startup claims all connections are unauthenticated
[gateway] auth mode=none explicitly configured; all gateway connections are unauthenticated.

# But backend clients still fail
gateway connect failed: GatewayClientRequestError: device identity required
[tools] sessions_list failed: gateway closed (1008): device identity required
```

## Solution

Use `gateway.auth.mode: "token"` instead of `"none"` when backend clients need to connect to the gateway.

## Changes

Added a note to the `gateway.auth.mode` documentation in `docs/gateway/configuration-reference.md` to clarify:

1. When set to `none`, the gateway does not generate a shared token
2. Backend clients will fail with `device identity required` errors
3. Recommend using `gateway.auth.mode: "token"` for backend client compatibility

## Testing

This behavior was observed and verified through actual testing:

| `gateway.auth.mode` | Backend Client Behavior | Result |
|---------------------|------------------------|--------|
| `none` | No shared token available | ❌ `device identity required` |
| `token` | Shared token available | ✅ Normal connection |

## Related

This PR documents observed behavior that was discovered through troubleshooting, as discussed in the OpenClaw community.

target.title: i18n: improve Indonesian Control UI labels
target.body:
﻿## Summary
- Improve Indonesian Control UI copy that was still English in the Agents, Debug, and exec approval panels.
- Keep placeholders unchanged for translated strings.
- Add a changelog entry for the user-facing locale fix.

## Verification
- `pnpm install --frozen-lockfile`
- `pnpm format:check ui/src/i18n/locales/id.ts CHANGELOG.md`
- `git diff --check origin/main...HEAD`

target.title: [codex] fix WhatsApp reconnect catch-up replies
target.body:
## Summary

- Keep the existing short stale-history guard for initial WhatsApp Web startup history sync.
- After reconnecting from a recently active WhatsApp listener, allow recent `messages.upsert(type="append")` catch-up messages to enter the auto-reply path within the existing message watchdog window.
- Add regression coverage for the inbox append grace window and the reconnect handoff from the Web monitor.
- Document the reconnect catch-up behavior in the WhatsApp runtime model and add a changelog entry.

## Root Cause

OpenClaw already received Baileys `append` upserts for history/offline catch-up, but `extensions/whatsapp/src/inbound/monitor.ts` only auto-replied when the message timestamp was within 60 seconds before the new socket's connect time. During a stale socket/reconnect window, a real inbound message can be several minutes older than the replacement socket's connect time. In that case OpenClaw marked the message read and skipped auto-reply as stale startup history.

Refs #50093.

## Verification

- `pnpm test extensions/whatsapp/src/monitor-inbox.behavior.test.ts extensions/whatsapp/src/auto-reply.web-auto-reply.connection-and-logging.e2e.test.ts`
- `pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 CHANGELOG.md docs/channels/whatsapp.md extensions/whatsapp/src/auto-reply.test-harness.ts extensions/whatsapp/src/auto-reply.web-auto-reply.connection-and-logging.e2e.test.ts extensions/whatsapp/src/auto-reply/monitor.ts extensions/whatsapp/src/inbound/monitor.ts extensions/whatsapp/src/monitor-inbox.append-upsert.test-support.ts`
- `git diff --check`
- `pnpm changed:lanes --json`
- `pnpm check:changed`

## Notes

I did not do a live WhatsApp-device replay because the original miss was observed on a personal local setup and GitHub auth required pushing through a fork. The focused tests cover the exact skipped `append` window and the reconnect option wiring.

target.title: Harden ACP worker dispatch controls
target.body:
## Summary

- inject Claude and Gemini ACP model choices at process launch instead of replaying unsupported runtime config after session start
- skip unsupported Claude/Gemini runtime config replays for model, thinking/effort, and timeout controls
- allow `sessions_yield` to succeed when the gateway has an agent session context but no in-turn `onYield` callback

## Context

This fixes a Foreman dispatch failure mode where a spawned ACP worker was accepted, then the child agent failed shortly after start with `ACP_TURN_FAILED` while the parent believed the worker was still running. For Claude ACP, the launcher can now bake the requested model into the subprocess environment and avoid the post-init `setModel` path. For Gemini ACP, the requested model is passed with `--model` at launch.

## Tests

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.extension-acpx.config.ts extensions/acpx/src/runtime.test.ts`
- `node scripts/test-projects.mjs src/agents/tools/sessions-yield-tool.test.ts`

target.title: fix(doctor): suppress --fix trailer when no pending config changes remain
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: `openclaw doctor` (without `--fix`) prints `Run "openclaw doctor --fix" to apply changes.` on every run, even when nothing in the run actually had a pending config change. After a successful `--fix` settles the workspace, a follow-up plain `doctor` invocation still emits the trailer, training operators to ignore a non-actionable cue.
- Why it matters: the trailer is the "you have something to apply" signal. When it fires on a clean state, it makes `Doctor complete.` ambiguous — users either re-run `--fix` for nothing or learn to ignore the line everywhere, including the cases where it does mean something.
- What changed: thread the already-tracked `pendingChanges` flag from `loadAndMaybeMigrateDoctorConfig` through the doctor config result, and gate the trailer in `runWriteConfigHealth` on it. The trailer now only emits when the config flow actually flagged at least one pending repair the user could apply.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): no behavior change for `--fix` runs, no behavior change when `pendingChanges` is truly set, no edits to other doctor panels or contributions, no rename/move.

## Change Type

- [x] Bug fix

## Scope

- [x] UI / DX (CLI diagnostics output)

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #80435
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior or issue addressed: the unconditional `Run "openclaw doctor --fix" to apply changes.` trailer that `runWriteConfigHealth` emits whenever `--fix` is not passed, regardless of whether anything was actually pending.
- Real environment tested: local macOS (Darwin 24.6.0, Node v24.10.0) checkout of `openclaw/openclaw` at `upstream/main`, run with `pnpm dev doctor`.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:
  - `pnpm install --frozen-lockfile`
  - `pnpm test src/flows/doctor-health-contributions.test.ts`
  - `pnpm check:changed` (lint core + tsgo core + tsgo core tests + import-cycles + boundary guards)
  - `OPENCLAW_HOME=/tmp/openclaw-trailer-test pnpm dev doctor --fix --non-interactive` (× 2 to settle)
  - `OPENCLAW_HOME=/tmp/openclaw-trailer-test pnpm dev doctor --non-interactive` (the previously failing invocation)
- Evidence after fix:
  - Unit lane: `Test Files 1 passed (1); Tests 11 passed (11)` — includes the two new regression cases for `doctor:write-config`.
  - `pnpm check:changed`: exits 0 with `Found 0 warnings and 0 errors.` on lint, clean typecheck on `tsgo:core` and `tsgo:core:test`, `0 runtime value cycle(s)` from `check:import-cycles`.
  - Settled clean re-run ends with `└  Doctor complete.` and no trailing `Run "openclaw doctor --fix" to apply changes.` line.
- Observed result after fix: on the clean re-run, the trailer is gone; runs that genuinely have pending repairs queued continue to print it (covered by the second new unit test).
- What was not tested: I did not reproduce the exact source-checkout + entrypoint-mismatch host layout from the bug report on this machine (no spare gateway-install/migration setup), so the live trailer-on-clean-state observation comes from a fresh `OPENCLAW_HOME` rather than the reporter's npm-over-pnpm upgrade scenario. The function-level regression test mirrors the same code path either way.

## Root Cause

- Root cause: `runWriteConfigHealth` reached its `else` branch whenever `shouldWriteConfig` resolved to `false`, and then emitted the trailer based only on whether `--fix` was missing. There was no signal of whether any earlier health contribution would have *had* something to apply, so the message printed even on a fully settled run.
- Missing detection / guardrail: there was no unit test asserting that the trailer is suppressed on a clean run. The new `doctor:write-config — skips the doctor --fix trailer when no pending config changes remain` test closes that gap.
- Contributing context (if known): `loadAndMaybeMigrateDoctorConfig` already tracks `pendingChanges` locally for `finalizeDoctorConfigFlow`, but it wasn't surfaced on the returned `DoctorConfigResult`, so downstream contributions had no way to consult it.

## Regression Test Plan

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
- Target test or file: `src/flows/doctor-health-contributions.test.ts`
- Scenarios locked in:
  - `doctor:write-config` skips the `--fix` trailer when `configResult.pendingChanges === false` and `prompter.shouldRepair === false`.
  - `doctor:write-config` still prints the `--fix` trailer when `configResult.pendingChanges === true` and `prompter.shouldRepair === false`.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: the bug lives entirely in the single branch in `runWriteConfigHealth`; exercising both `pendingChanges` values against the live `doctor:write-config` contribution is enough to lock in the gate without standing up the whole doctor flow.
- Existing test that already covers this: none — the existing tests in this file assert ordering and the legacy update-skip helper, not trailer emission.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

`openclaw doctor` (no `--fix`) no longer prints `Run "openclaw doctor --fix" to apply changes.` on runs where no health contribution flagged a pending config change. Runs that do have pending changes are unchanged.

## Diagram

```text
Before:
doctor --non-interactive (clean state) -> Doctor complete. + "Run openclaw doctor --fix to apply changes."

After:
doctor --non-interactive (clean state) -> Doctor complete.
doctor --non-interactive (pending change) -> Doctor complete. + "Run openclaw doctor --fix to apply changes."
```

## Security Impact

- New permissions/capabilities? No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? No
- New/changed network calls? No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? No
- Data access scope changed? No

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS (Darwin 24.6.0), reporter saw it on Ubuntu 24.04 aarch64
- Runtime/container: Node v24.10.0 (reporter: Node v22.22.2)
- Model/provider: N/A — pure doctor CLI diagnostics
- Integration/channel (if any): N/A
- Relevant config (redacted): a settled `~/.openclaw/openclaw.json` where the previous `--fix` exited without `Updated ~/.openclaw/openclaw.json`

### Steps

1. `openclaw doctor --fix --non-interactive` until it exits cleanly with no `Updated ~/.openclaw/openclaw.json` line.
2. `openclaw doctor --non-interactive` immediately afterward.
3. Observe the trailer.

### Expected

The settled re-run ends with `└  Doctor complete.` and no trailing `Run "openclaw doctor --fix" to apply changes.` line.

### Actual (pre-fix)

The settled re-run ends with `Run "openclaw doctor --fix" to apply changes.` followed by `└  Doctor complete.`, even though no panel reported a pending change.

## Evidence

- [x] Failing-then-passing unit assertions covering both `pendingChanges` branches (`src/flows/doctor-health-contributions.test.ts`).
- [x] Trace/log snippet from `pnpm dev doctor --non-interactive` on settled `OPENCLAW_HOME` showing `Doctor complete.` with no trailer.

## Human Verification

- Verified scenarios:
  - Settled clean re-run of `pnpm dev doctor --non-interactive` against a freshly populated `OPENCLAW_HOME` ends with `Doctor complete.` and no trailer.
  - `pnpm test src/flows/doctor-health-contributions.test.ts` — 11 passed, including the two new cases.
  - `pnpm check:changed` — green on lint core, runtime sidecar loader guard, import-cycles, webhook body guard, pairing guards, changelog attributions, dup coverage, `tsgo:core`, `tsgo:core:test`, oxlint core.
- Edge cases checked:
  - `pendingChanges === undefined` (older test mocks / future callers) — falsy, trailer suppressed; backwards compatible with existing inline-context tests in this file.
  - `pendingChanges === true && shouldRepair === false` — trailer still prints (covered by the second new unit test).
  - `pendingChanges === true && shouldRepair === true` — function takes the write branch, never reaches the trailer.
- What I did **not** verify: a live reproduction of the original Ubuntu 24.04 aarch64 layout with `auditGatewayServiceConfig` returning a non-empty issue list, because that requires the reporter's npm-over-pnpm upgrade setup.

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? Yes — `pendingChanges` is added as an optional field on `DoctorConfigResult`; existing mocks/test fixtures that omit it observe the previous "no trailer" outcome they already had when nothing was pending.
- Config/env changes? No
- Migration needed? No

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: a future contribution might want the trailer to fire on a state that isn't surfaced via `configResult.pendingChanges`.
  - Mitigation: any new pending state should be threaded through `loadAndMaybeMigrateDoctorConfig` (now trivial — just OR it into the existing local `pendingChanges`). The regression test locks the contract for `runWriteConfigHealth`.

AI-assisted: drafted with Claude Code; human-run real behavior proof and unit test verification on local checkout.

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)

The PR allows selected service-managed `~/.openclaw/.env` keys to survive into trusted gateway exec, documents that behavior, adds targeted env/security tests, and adds `--force-exclude` to the OpenGrep wrapper.

PR surface: Source +65, Tests +502, Docs +12, Other 0. Total +579 across 15 files.

Reproducibility: yes. source-level and media proof are strong: current main strips `GH_TOKEN`-class keys from both durable dotenv and inherited exec env, while the inspected before/after screenshots show the managed-service exec path failing before and working after the patch.
commands.review_followup: - [P2] The remaining blocker is maintainer judgment on the trusted-exec secret boundary, not a narrow code repair that automation can safely choose.

target.title: fix(env): propagate service-managed GH_TOKEN to trusted exec
target.body: ## Summary - Problem: `~/.openclaw/.env` could not be used as a durable source for operator-curated tool secrets like `GH_TOKEN` because override-only-dangerous keys were stripped before the managed gateway service could expose them to trusted exec children. - Why it matters: operators can configure `gh` and similar CLI tools for OpenClaw, but after gateway install/restart those tools could not see `GH_TOKEN` through the managed service path, so authenticated CLI workflows failed unless the token was injected some other way. - What changed: allow `~/.openclaw/.env` to retain override-only-blocked secrets as managed service keys, add a trusted exec inherited-env allowlist sourced from `OPENCLAW_SERVICE_MANAGED_ENV_KEYS`, and document the durable service env behavior. - What did NOT change (scope boundary): everywhere-dangerous env vars such as `LD_PRELOAD`, `NODE_OPTIONS`, `BASH_ENV`, `DYLD_*`, and `GIT_DIR` stay blocked. `OPENCLAW_*` keys still do not gain trusted exec inheritance. Untrusted exec postures do not receive these secrets. ## Change Type (select all) - [x] Bug fix - [ ] Feature - [x] Refactor required for the fix - [x] Docs - [x] Security hardening - [ ] Chore/infra ## Scope (select all touched areas) - [x] Gateway / orchestration - [x] Skills / tool execution - [x] Auth / tokens - [ ] Memory / storage - [ ] Integrations - [ ] API / contracts - [x] UI / DX - [ ] CI/CD / infra ## Linked Issue/PR - Closes # - Related # - [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression ## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs) - Behavior or issue addressed: `GH_TOKEN` placed in `~/.openclaw/.env` should survive managed gateway install/restart and be available to trusted gateway exec children, so `gh` can authenticate without manually injecting the token into each command. - Real environment tested: macOS OpenClaw gateway installed as a managed service, local OpenClaw checkout from v2026.5.2 with this patch applied, `GH_TOKEN` present in `~/.openclaw/.env`, Telegram/agent exec path using `host=gateway`, `security=full`, `ask=off`. - Exact steps or command run after this patch: ```sh cp ~/.env ~/.openclaw/.env chmod 600 ~/.openclaw/.env openclaw gateway stop openclaw gateway install --force openclaw gateway start openclaw gateway status ``` then ask the agent to run `gh auth status` and report back - Evidence after fix (screenshot): - Telegram screenshot showing the live exec environment reporting `GH_TOKEN_present: true`, `service_managed_keys_contains_GH_TOKEN: true`, `gh auth status` logged in via `GH_TOKEN`, and `gh api user` returning the authenticated account JSON. Account details and token values are redacted. - Observed result after fix: trusted gateway exec can use `GH_TOKEN` inherited from the managed service env, and `gh` authenticates successfully from the OpenClaw exec environment. - What was not tested: Linux/systemd real-service behavior was not manually tested; the Linux path is covered by the shared service-env/unit tests. Non-trusted exec postures were verified by targeted unit tests, not by a second live service screenshot. The exec integration test (`bash-tools.exec-trusted-env.exec.test.ts`) is skipped on Windows (shell-dependent); unit-level coverage runs on all platforms. - Before evidence (optional but encouraged): - `before-1.jpg` and `before-2.jpg` — terminal screenshots showing the pre-fix managed-service behavior where the gateway env path stripped `GH_TOKEN` before it could reach the exec child, so `gh` could not authenticate after service install/restart. ## Root Cause (if applicable) - Root cause: `~/.openclaw/.env` parsing treated keys that are dangerous as agent request overrides the same as keys that are dangerous everywhere. This stripped operator-curated tool tokens like `GH_TOKEN` before the managed service could record them as durable env. Separately, exec env sanitization had no trusted-mode inherited-key allowlist, so there was no narrow path for managed service keys to reach trusted tool children. - Missing detection / guardrail: existing tests covered dangerous env stripping and service env formatting, but did not cover the desired operator-curated secret path from `~/.openclaw/.env` through managed service metadata into trusted gateway exec. - Contributing context (if known): the security model already distinguished inherited/env override risk, but the durable service env path did not preserve that distinction. ## Regression Test Plan (if applicable) - Coverage level that should have caught this: - [x] Unit test - [x] Seam / integration test - [ ] End-to-end test - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient - Target test or file: - `src/config/state-dir-dotenv.test.ts` - `src/daemon/service-managed-env.test.ts` - `src/infra/host-env-security.test.ts` - `src/agents/bash-tools.exec-trusted-env.test.ts` - `src/agents/bash-tools.exec-trusted-env.exec.test.ts` - `src/commands/daemon-install-helpers.test.ts` - Scenario the test should lock in: `GH_TOKEN` and similar override-only-blocked keys can be retained from owner-controlled durable service env, become managed service keys, and reach exec children only in the trusted gateway posture (`host=gateway`, `security=full`, `ask=off`). Unsafe keys and `OPENCLAW_*` keys remain blocked. - Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: unit tests cover each policy seam, and the exec integration test proves the env-composition wiring reaches the child process under the relevant exec postures without requiring live GitHub credentials in CI. - Existing test that already covers this (if any): N/A. - If no new test is added, why not: N/A, new tests were added. ## User-visible / Behavior Changes Operators can place `GH_TOKEN` in `~/.openclaw/.env`, reinstall/start the managed gateway, and use `gh` from trusted gateway exec. This does not grant those tokens to untrusted exec postures. ## Diagram (if applicable) ```text Before: ~/.openclaw/.env GH_TOKEN -> durable env parser strips override-only-dangerous key -> managed gateway service has no GH_TOKEN-managed allowlist entry -> trusted exec child cannot authenticate gh After: ~/.openclaw/.env GH_TOKEN -> durable env parser keeps operator-curated override-only key -> install records GH_TOKEN in OPENCLAW_SERVICE_MANAGED_ENV_KEYS -> trusted gateway exec allowlist permits inherited GH_TOKEN -> gh authenticates from the exec child ``` ## Security Impact (required) - New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) No. - Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) Yes. - New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) No. - Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) Yes. - Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) Yes. - If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: this intentionally allows operator-curated service env secrets like `GH_TOKEN` to reach trusted gateway exec children. The path is constrained to keys recorded by the managed service env metadata and to the trusted exec posture. Everywhere-dangerous keys are still stripped, `OPENCLAW_*` keys are excluded, request overrides remain sanitized, and non-trusted postures do not inherit the allowlisted secret. ## Repro + Verification ### Environment - OS: macOS - Runtime/container: local managed OpenClaw gateway service - Model/provider: N/A - Integration/channel (if any): OpenClaw agent exec path, GitHub CLI (`gh`) as the real CLI consumer - Relevant config (redacted): `GH_TOKEN` present in `~/.openclaw/.env`; `OPENCLAW_SERVICE_MANAGED_ENV_KEYS` included `GH_TOKEN`; token value redacted in all evidence. ### Steps 1. Put `GH_TOKEN` in `~/.openclaw/.env` and set owner-only permissions. 2. Reinstall/restart the managed gateway service with this patch applied. 3. Run a trusted gateway exec env probe and `gh auth status` / `gh api user`. 4. Run the targeted regression tests: ```sh pnpm test src/daemon/service-managed-env.test.ts src/agents/bash-tools.exec-trusted-env.test.ts src/agents/bash-tools.exec-trusted-env.exec.test.ts src/config/state-dir-dotenv.test.ts src/infra/host-env-security.test.ts src/commands/daemon-install-helpers.test.ts ``` ### Expected - `process.env.GH_TOKEN` is present inside trusted gateway exec. - `OPENCLAW_SERVICE_MANAGED_ENV_KEYS` contains `GH_TOKEN`. - `gh auth status` reports authentication via `GH_TOKEN`. - Untrusted exec postures do not receive the fixture token in tests. - Everywhere-dangerous and `OPENCLAW_*` keys remain blocked. ### Actual - Live evidence showed `GH_TOKEN_present: true` and `service_managed_keys_contains_GH_TOKEN: true`. - `gh auth status` authenticated via `GH_TOKEN` and `gh api user` succeeded with redacted account output. - Targeted tests covered the parser, managed service allowlist, host env sanitizer, daemon install plan, and exec wiring. ## Evidence Attach at least one: - [x] Failing test/log before + passing after - [ ] Trace/log snippets - [x] Screenshot/recording - [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant) Evidence files prepared for upload: - `before-1.jpg` - `before-2.jpg` - `after-1.jpg` ## Human Verification (required) What you personally verified (not just CI), and how: - Verified scenarios: - Managed gateway install/restart picks up `GH_TOKEN` from `~/.openclaw/.env`. - Trusted gateway exec sees `GH_TOKEN` and `OPENCLAW_SERVICE_MANAGED_ENV_KEYS` includes `GH_TOKEN`. - `gh auth status` and `gh api user` work from the OpenClaw exec environment using `GH_TOKEN`. - Targeted tests exercise trusted, allowlist, and prompted exec postures plus dangerous-key exclusions. - Edge cases checked: - `OPENCLAW_*` keys are excluded from trusted inherited allowlist. - Everywhere-dangerous keys remain blocked even if listed. - PATH override smuggling remains blocked. - Default sanitizer behavior remains strict without `allowInheritedKeys`. - What you did **not** verify: - Manual Linux/systemd live service verification. - Every provider token named in the override-only dangerous list; `GH_TOKEN` is the live proof case and tests use representative fixture keys. ## Review Conversations - [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR. - [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment. If a bot review conversation is addressed by this PR, resolve that conversation yourself. Do not leave bot review conversation cleanup for maintainers. ## Compatibility / Migration - Backward compatible? (`Yes/No`) Yes. - Config/env changes? (`Yes/No`) Yes. - Migration needed? (`Yes/No`) Yes, for operators who want this behavior. - If yes, exact upgrade steps: place `GH_TOKEN` or similar operator-curated tool secret in `~/.openclaw/.env`, ensure the file is owner-only, then reinstall/restart the managed gateway service so `OPENCLAW_SERVICE_MANAGED_ENV_KEYS` is regenerated. ## Risks and Mitigations - Risk: secrets that were previously stripped from managed service env can now reach trusted exec children. - Mitigation: only operator-curated durable service env keys are considered, only trusted gateway exec posture receives the inherited allowlist, and dangerous-everywhere plus `OPENCLAW_*` keys remain blocked. - Risk: operators may assume all exec postures receive `GH_TOKEN` after placing it in `~/.openclaw/.env`. - Mitigation: docs state that the exec layer decides inheritance by posture and that these keys are not auto-propagated to untrusted commands.
