target.title: feat(mattermost): add automatic ack reactions
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: Mattermost supported typing, preview streaming, and manual `message action=react`, but it did not send automatic ack reactions for accepted inbound messages.
- Why it matters: Mattermost was the main chat-channel outlier versus Discord, Slack, Telegram, Matrix, and WhatsApp for the baseline "seen / working on it" feedback path.
- What changed: added Mattermost automatic ack reactions, Mattermost emoji-name normalization for common unicode defaults, optional `channels.mattermost.ackReaction` / `channels.mattermost.accounts.<id>.ackReaction` overrides, and the matching generated config-surface artifacts.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): no Mattermost lifecycle status reactions, no changes to typing behavior, and no changes to draft preview streaming.

## Change Type (select all)

- [ ] Bug fix
- [x] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [x] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [x] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #
- Related #
- [ ] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: automatic Mattermost ack reactions for accepted inbound messages, including config override resolution and optional ack cleanup after reply.
- Real environment tested: local OpenClaw maintainer worktree on Linux with Mattermost-focused tests, build, generated-config checks, the full changed-scope gate, and a live Mattermost workspace smoke on this exact branch.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:
  - `pnpm test extensions/mattermost/src/config-schema.test.ts extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/reactions.test.ts extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/ack-reactions.test.ts`
  - `pnpm build`
  - `pnpm check:bundled-channel-config-metadata`
  - `pnpm config:docs:check`
  - `pnpm check:changed`
- Evidence after fix (screenshot, recording, terminal capture, console output, redacted runtime log, linked artifact, or copied live output):
```text
Test Files  3 passed (3)
Tests  20 passed (20)

pnpm build -> passed
pnpm check:bundled-channel-config-metadata -> passed
pnpm config:docs:check -> OK docs/.generated/config-baseline.sha256
pnpm check:changed -> passed

Live Mattermost smoke on this branch -> passed
- started the branch with `pnpm gateway:watch`
- configured `messages.ackReactionScope=all` and `channels.mattermost.ackReaction=eyes`
- sent real Mattermost messages in the connected workspace
- observed visible ack reactions in Mattermost
- no ack/reaction failure strings appeared in the runtime logs
```
- Observed result after fix: Mattermost now has an automatic ack reaction path wired into inbound monitoring, common unicode ack emoji are normalized to Mattermost emoji names, config/docs/generated surfaces are aligned, and visible ack reactions were confirmed in a live Mattermost workspace on this exact branch.
- What was not tested: exhaustive live coverage for uncommon/custom unicode emoji names.
- Before evidence (optional but encouraged): before this patch, Mattermost only supported manual `message action=react`; there was no automatic inbound ack reaction path in the monitor.

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause: N/A. This is a scoped feature/parity improvement, not a regression fix.
- Missing detection / guardrail: the first implementation pass missed the generated bundled channel metadata and config baseline hash that need to move with a public config-surface change.
- Contributing context (if known): Mattermost has a preview-streaming-first design, so this change intentionally stops at the ack layer instead of adding full status-reaction lifecycle UI.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [ ] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: `extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/ack-reactions.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in: account/channel/global ack resolution, mention-scope gating, emoji normalization, and `removeAckAfterReply` cleanup behavior.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: the existing Mattermost websocket monitor harness hangs in this worktree, so the smallest stable coverage is the new ack helper unit coverage plus generated-surface/build/changed-gate proof.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): `extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/reactions.test.ts` now covers emoji normalization through the reaction transport.
- If no new test is added, why not: a monitor-level websocket assertion was attempted and dropped because the existing harness hangs; I kept stable unit coverage rather than land a flaky test.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- Mattermost can now add automatic ack reactions for accepted inbound messages.
- `messages.removeAckAfterReply` now applies to Mattermost ack reactions too.
- Mattermost now supports optional `channels.mattermost.ackReaction` and `channels.mattermost.accounts.<id>.ackReaction` overrides.
- Common unicode ack emoji like `👀` are normalized to Mattermost emoji names automatically.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
[inbound Mattermost message] -> [mention/access gating] -> [typing + preview streaming] -> [reply]

After:
[inbound Mattermost message] -> [mention/access gating] -> [auto ack reaction] -> [typing + preview streaming] -> [reply] -> [optional ack removal]
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`No`)
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`No`)
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes`)
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`No`)
- Data access scope changed? (`No`)
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation:
  - The new network calls are the existing Mattermost reaction add/remove API calls, sent only for accepted inbound messages when ack reactions are enabled. They are gated by normal Mattermost access checks, `messages.ackReaction*`, and `channels.mattermost.actions.reactions`.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Linux
- Runtime/container: local Node 24 worktree
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel (if any): Mattermost
- Relevant config (redacted): `messages.ackReaction`, `messages.ackReactionScope`, `messages.removeAckAfterReply`, optional `channels.mattermost.ackReaction`

### Steps

1. Configure `messages.ackReaction` and `messages.ackReactionScope` for Mattermost.
2. Send an accepted inbound Mattermost message that passes normal access/mention gating.
3. Observe ack reaction creation, then send a reply path that optionally removes the ack when `removeAckAfterReply` is enabled.

### Expected

- Mattermost adds the configured ack reaction when the inbound message qualifies.
- Common unicode defaults normalize to Mattermost emoji names.
- Ack cleanup only happens after a visible reply is delivered.

### Actual

- Verified via new unit coverage, generated-surface checks, build, `pnpm check:changed`, and a live Mattermost smoke on this exact branch that showed visible ack reactions plus live ack removal after visible replies.

## Evidence

Attach at least one:

- [ ] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [x] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

What you personally verified (not just CI), and how:

- Verified scenarios: config override resolution, mention-scope gating, common emoji normalization, `removeAckAfterReply`, `actions.reactions=false`, generated config metadata, config baseline hash, build, the full changed-scope gate, live Mattermost ack creation on this exact branch, and live ack removal after visible replies.
- Edge cases checked: no ack when the message is dropped before dispatch, no ack removal when no reply is delivered, and no default-group ack for open `chatmode: "onmessage"` rooms when scope remains `group-mentions`.
- What you did **not** verify: uncommon/custom emoji names beyond the common normalized set.

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes`)
- Config/env changes? (`Yes`)
- Migration needed? (`No`)
- If yes, exact upgrade steps:

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk:
  - Mattermost shortcode normalization is not exhaustive for every possible unicode emoji.
  - Mitigation:
    - Common/default ack emoji are normalized explicitly, and raw shortcode config still works.
- Risk:
  - Live proof does not exhaustively cover uncommon/custom emoji names.
  - Mitigation:
    - Added focused unit coverage, ran build, refreshed generated config artifacts, passed `pnpm check:changed`, confirmed live ack creation and live cleanup after visible replies, and verified that `NO_REPLY` leaves the ack in place as expected.

I did not run a physical-device reproduction in this read-only review.

target.title: feat(android): add chat agent selector
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: the Android chat surface could show multiple agents indirectly, but users had no explicit way to choose which configured gateway agent should receive new chat messages.
- Why it matters: multi-agent OpenClaw setups need an obvious mobile path for switching between agents such as `main` and local agents without relying on existing session history or hidden session-key behavior.
- What changed: the Android runtime now exposes gateway agent summaries to chat UI state, and the chat sheet renders an agent selector chip row above the session/thread selector.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): no gateway API contract changes, no new Android permissions, no authentication changes, and no changes to session/thread listing semantics beyond switching the active phone chat session key when an agent is selected.

## Change Type (select all)

- [ ] Bug fix
- [x] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [x] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #
- Related #
- [ ] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: Android Chat now displays selectable agent chips when the connected gateway reports more than one agent, allowing mobile users to switch between agents before sending messages.
- Real environment tested: local OpenClaw gateway with multiple configured agents (`main` plus a local Ollama-backed agent), Android debug build generated from this branch and sideloaded/tested on a physical Android phone.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:
  1. Build Android debug APK from this branch.
  2. Install/sideload the debug APK on the Android phone.
  3. Connect the app to the local OpenClaw gateway.
  4. Open Chat and verify the agent selector row appears above the session chips.
  5. Select the alternate agent and send a chat message.
- Evidence after fix (screenshot, recording, terminal capture, console output, redacted runtime log, linked artifact, or copied live output):
  - Screenshot from the physical Android phone after this patch: ![Screenshot_20260510-154513.png](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/0637284d-e61c-41b8-a0bb-edf635a94893)
  - Manual runtime verification on a real Android phone confirmed the selector appeared and worked.
  - Copied live build/lint output from the patched branch is included below.
- Observed result after fix: Chat showed agent selector chips and selecting another agent switched the chat target for subsequent messages.
- What was not tested: automated UI screenshot tests and full end-to-end CI device tests were not added.
- Before evidence (optional but encouraged): before this patch, Android Chat exposed session chips only; there was no first-class agent selector in the Chat sheet.

## Root Cause (if applicable)

N/A — this is a feature addition, not a bug fix or regression.

- If no new test is added, why not: this is a small UI wiring change using existing gateway `agents.list` data already fetched by `NodeRuntime`; it was manually verified on a real device and covered by Android lint/build gates.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

Android Chat now shows an agent selector row when the connected gateway reports multiple agents. Tapping an agent chip switches the active phone chat target to that agent’s node session before sending messages.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
[open Android Chat] -> [session/thread chips only] -> [no direct agent choice]

After:
[open Android Chat] -> [agent chips + session/thread chips]
                    -> [tap agent chip]
                    -> [chat target switches to selected agent]
```

## Security Impact (required)

## Repro + Verification

### Steps

1. Build the Android debug APK from this branch.
2. Install/sideload the APK on an Android phone connected to an OpenClaw gateway with multiple agents configured.
3. Open the Android app Chat surface.
4. Confirm agent chips appear above session/thread chips.
5. Tap a non-default agent chip and send a message.

### Expected

- Android Chat provides a visible way to select the desired gateway agent.
- Selecting an agent switches the active phone chat target to that agent.
- Existing session/thread selection still works below the agent selector.

### Actual

- Agent chips render above session/thread chips when multiple agents are available.
- Selecting another agent switches the chat target and messages route to that agent.
- Existing chat session controls remain available.

```

```

## Human Verification (required)

What you personally verified (not just CI), and how:

- Verified scenarios:

  - Android Chat agent selector appeared in a real multi-agent setup.
  - Selecting the alternate agent switched the active chat target before sending.
- Edge cases checked:

If a bot review conversation is addressed by this PR, resolve that conversation yourself. Do not leave bot review conversation cleanup for maintainers.

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: the extra chip row could add vertical density to the Chat sheet on small screens.
 - Mitigation: the row uses the same compact horizontal-scroll chip pattern as the existing session selector and is hidden unless multiple agents are available.
- Risk: selecting an agent could be confused with selecting a session/thread.
 - Mitigation: the agent selector is rendered as a separate row above the existing session chips and uses the gateway agent name/emoji where available.

target.title: refactor(auto-reply): drop dead /reset branches in fast-path session init
target.body:
## Summary

This PR was originally proposed as a fix for #80377, claiming `/reset` was preserving session-level overrides incorrectly. After review by `clawsweeper` and inspection of `initSessionState` (the actual production reset path), it turns out the runtime behavior is **intentional**:

- behavior overrides (thinking, verbose, reasoning, ttsAuto) are deliberately preserved across `/reset` so users don't re-enable them every time (see `src/auto-reply/reply/session.ts:510-518`)
- model/provider/auth overrides go through `resolveResetPreservedSelection`, which preserves user-driven selections and clears only auto-created ones (per #69301)

So this PR has been **pivoted from a behavior fix to a cleanup-only refactor**. The original commit (`1a1685a`) and the regression tests it added have been reverted in `1d70235`. The only remaining net change versus `origin/main` is replacing nine trivial `resetTriggered ? existingEntry?.X : existingEntry?.X` ternaries in `initFastReplySessionState` with the simple `existingEntry?.X` expression.

**Net effect: zero semantic change vs `origin/main`.** The dead `resetTriggered` branches no longer mislead readers into thinking the fast-path bootstrap has reset-specific logic that the runtime doesn't actually have.

Issue #80377 should be closed as wontfix / by-design (the bundle pattern that was reported is a copy-paste artifact in this fast-path init, not a production runtime bug). This PR does **not** fix #80377; it only references it.

## Files touched

- `src/auto-reply/reply/get-reply-fast-path.ts` — simplify 9 no-op ternaries (no semantic change)

## Behavior proof (no-op refactor)

This is a semantic no-op cleanup; the proof is the diff itself. Before/after, every assignment evaluates to the same value:

```ts
// Before (one of nine identical patterns)
thinkingLevel: resetTriggered ? existingEntry?.thinkingLevel : existingEntry?.thinkingLevel,
// After
thinkingLevel: existingEntry?.thinkingLevel,
```

For both `resetTriggered === true` and `resetTriggered === false`, both expressions yield `existingEntry?.thinkingLevel`. The `resetTriggered` semantics still flow through the `...(!resetTriggered ? existingEntry : undefined)` spread on line 245 (unchanged), so reset still drops `responseUsage` and `sessionStartedAt` correctly. The full pre-existing test suite for `get-reply.fast-path.test.ts` (12 tests) continues to pass:

```
Test Files  1 passed (1)
     Tests  12 passed (12)
```

## Test plan

- [x] `pnpm test src/auto-reply/reply/get-reply.fast-path.test.ts` → 12/12 (pre-existing tests, unchanged)
- [x] `pnpm tsgo:core` → green
- [x] `pnpm tsgo:core:test` → green (with `GOMEMLIMIT=2GiB` on a memory-constrained dev box)

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)

target.title: feat(agents): per-agent model allowlist (with fallback to global)
target.body:
When `agents.list[<agentId>].models` is defined and non-empty, scope the visibility allowlist (used by the Control UI model picker, `/model`, and the gateway `models.list` catalog view) to that agent. Otherwise fall back to `agents.defaults.models` exactly as before.

Schema:
- `agents.list[].models` already validated via the existing `AgentEntrySchema` Zod block; reuses the same shape as `agents.defaults.models` (`alias`, `params`, `agentRuntime`, `streaming`).
- gateway protocol `models.list` params: optional `agentId` (NonEmptyString).

Behavior:
- `buildAllowedModelSetWithFallbacks` / `parseConfiguredModelVisibilityEntries` now accept `agentId`; aliases and metadata still resolve from defaults.
- gateway `server-methods/models` honors `models.list { agentId }` and threads it into the visibility filter and configured-view catalog loader.
- ui controllers/`loadModels` and slash-command executor pass the active session's `agentId` so model pickers show the per-agent list.

Backwards compatibility:
- No `agentId`, unknown `agentId`, or empty per-agent `models` => identical behavior to today (defaults.models drives the allowlist).
- `agents.defaults.models` semantics, aliases, and provider wildcards are unchanged. `agents.list[].models` entries can also use `provider/*` wildcards.

Tests:
- `src/agents/model-selection.test.ts`: 7 new cases covering per-agent allowlist, fallback to defaults when missing/unknown/empty, provider wildcards, and policy surfacing.
- `src/agents/model-catalog-visibility.test.ts`: end-to-end visibility scoping via the public model-selection API.
- `src/gateway/server-methods/models.test.ts`: gateway `models.list` filter with `agentId`.
- `src/gateway/protocol/index.test.ts`: protocol schema accepts `agentId`.
- `ui/src/ui/controllers/models.test.ts`: client passes `agentId` through.
- `ui/src/ui/app-chat.test.ts` and `slash-command-executor.node.test.ts`: session-scoped catalog refresh forwards the agent id.

Docs:
- `docs/gateway/config-agents.md`: documents per-agent allowlist semantics, fallback rules, and an example config.

## Summary

- **Problem**: The visibility allowlist that powers the Control UI model picker, `/model`, and the gateway `models.list` configured view is global — driven exclusively by `agents.defaults.models`. Multi-agent setups cannot scope which models appear in each agent's picker.
- **Why it matters**: With multi-agent setups (e.g. writer / researcher / coder split), every agent's picker shows the same list, leaking irrelevant options into agents that should never see them.
- **What changed**: `agents.list[<agentId>].models`, when present and non-empty, now scopes the allowlist to that agent. Threaded `agentId` through the model-selection helpers, the gateway `models.list` server method, the protocol schema, and the UI catalog loaders.
- **What did NOT change (scope boundary)**: When `agentId` is absent/unknown or the per-agent `models` map is empty, behavior is bit-for-bit identical to today. `agents.defaults.models` semantics, aliases, provider wildcards, and any other model resolution logic remain untouched.

## Change Type (select all)

- [ ] Bug fix
- [x] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [x] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [x] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #
- Related #
- [ ] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: Today the Control UI model picker, `/model`, and the gateway `models.list` configured view are driven exclusively by `agents.defaults.models` (global). With this change, when `agents.list[<agentId>].models` is defined and non-empty, the picker for that agent is scoped to that list; otherwise it falls back to the global allowlist exactly as before.
- **Real environment tested**: OpenClaw built locally from this branch (commit `5973f9d0c7`, version `2026.5.10-beta.1`) on Linux (WSL2), Node v22.22.2. Gateway running in an isolated `--profile pr-evidence` on port 18790 against a multi-agent config that exercises both the new per-agent allowlist and the fallback path. Productive install on the standard port left untouched.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**: ran `node openclaw.mjs --profile pr-evidence gateway --port 18790` after `git checkout feat/per-agent-model-allowlist` (commit `5973f9d0c7`) and a full `pnpm install` followed by `pnpm build`. Then opened `http://localhost:18790` in a real browser and switched between the three agents (`Claw`, `El Becario`, `El Gestor`) inspecting each model dropdown.
- **Evidence after fix**: live Control UI screenshots from the running gateway. Before / after comparison embedded inline. Before, productive install on `2026.5.7` (no per-agent allowlist support), both agents render the same 9-model dropdown — Claw: ![claw-baseline](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/pleyvah/openclaw/pr-evidence-80394/assets/before-claw-dropdown.png) and El Becario: ![becario-baseline](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/pleyvah/openclaw/pr-evidence-80394/assets/before-becario-dropdown.png). After, built from this branch with per-agent `agents.list[].models` set on Claw (3 models) and El Becario (2 models), each dropdown is now scoped — Claw: ![claw-after](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/pleyvah/openclaw/pr-evidence-80394/assets/after-claw-dropdown.png) and El Becario: ![becario-after](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/pleyvah/openclaw/pr-evidence-80394/assets/after-becario-dropdown.png).
- **Observed result after fix**: Live observation in the running OpenClaw browser session: agents that declare `agents.list[].models` see only their own subset in the Control UI dropdown; agents without that field still see the full `agents.defaults.models` catalog. Switching between agents in the same browser session updates the dropdown contents accordingly, confirming that the UI controllers thread `agentId` through to `models.list` correctly. Concretely the Control UI rendered: `Claw` showed exactly its 3 declared models (`anthropic/claude-opus-4-7`, `openai-codex/gpt-5.5`, `ollama/gemma4:26b`); `El Becario` showed exactly its 2 (`ollama/gemma4:26b`, `ollama/devstral-small-2:latest`); `El Gestor` (no override) still showed the full 9-model global catalog. Live screenshot of Claw scoped picker: ![claw-after-2](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/pleyvah/openclaw/pr-evidence-80394/assets/after-claw-dropdown.png) and live screenshot of El Becario scoped picker: ![becario-after-2](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/pleyvah/openclaw/pr-evidence-80394/assets/after-becario-dropdown.png).
- **What was not tested**: TUI surface (the change paths through `ui/src/ui/chat/slash-command-executor.ts` which the TUI also consumes, but the live screenshots are web-only); external (non-OpenClaw) consumers of the gateway JSON-RPC protocol (`agentId` is optional in the schema, so omitting it preserves the previous behavior); macOS/iOS Swift clients (`apps/shared/OpenClawKit/Sources/OpenClawProtocol/GatewayModels.swift` was regenerated via `pnpm protocol:gen:swift` to include `agentid: String?` in `ModelsListParams`, but a live Swift consumer test was not run).

Config used (illustrative, redacted):

```jsonc
{
  "agents": {
    "defaults": {
      "models": {
        "openai-codex/gpt-5.5": {},
        "ollama/gemma4:26b": {},
        "ollama/qwen3-coder:30b": {},
        "ollama/qwen2.5-coder:32b": {},
        "ollama/devstral-small-2:latest": {},
        "anthropic/claude-opus-4-7": {},
        "claude-cli/claude-opus-4-7": {},
        "claude-cli/claude-sonnet-4-6": {},
        "claude-cli/claude-haiku-4-5": {}
      }
    },
    "list": [
      {
        "id": "main",
        "identity": { "name": "Claw" },
        "models": {
          "anthropic/claude-opus-4-7": {},
          "openai-codex/gpt-5.5": {},
          "ollama/gemma4:26b": {}
        }
      },
      {
        "id": "el-becario",
        "identity": { "name": "El Becario" },
        "models": {
          "ollama/gemma4:26b": {},
          "ollama/devstral-small-2:latest": {}
        }
      },
      { "id": "el-gestor", "identity": { "name": "El Gestor" } }
    ]
  }
}
```


## Root Cause (if applicable)

N/A — this is a feature, not a regression.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

N/A — not a regression. New behavior is covered by the unit and integration tests listed above.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- New optional config field: `agents.list[].models` (same shape as `agents.defaults.models`).
- Control UI model picker, `/model` command, and `models.list` configured view become per-agent when that field is set; behavior is unchanged when it is absent or empty.
- No default config changes — existing installs see no difference until they opt in.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
  [any agent] -> models.list (no agentId) -> defaults.models -> dropdown

After:
  [agent A with per-agent models] -> models.list { agentId: A } -> list[A].models -> dropdown
  [agent B without per-agent models] -> models.list { agentId: B } -> defaults.models (fallback) -> dropdown
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? **No**
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? **No**
- New/changed network calls? **No**
- Command/tool execution surface changed? **No**
- Data access scope changed? **No** (the new field narrows visibility further; it never broadens access)

## Repro + Verification

### Environment
- OS: Linux (WSL2, kernel 6.6.87.2-microsoft-standard-WSL2)
- Runtime/container: Node v22.22.2, OpenClaw 2026.5.7 local install
- Model/provider: tested with anthropic, openai-codex, ollama, claude-cli entries
- Integration/channel (if any): webchat (Control UI)
- Relevant config (redacted): multi-agent config with `agents.defaults.models` populated and one agent overriding via `agents.list[].models`.

### Steps
1. Apply patch, build, run tests.
2. Configure one agent with a per-agent `models` map and leave another untouched.
3. Switch agents in Control UI and inspect the model dropdown contents.

### Expected
- Agent with override: dropdown lists only the agent's models.
- Agent without override: dropdown lists `agents.defaults.models`.

### Actual
- Matches expected. All targeted test suites pass.

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after (new tests added; pre-existing suites unchanged)
- [ ] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

- **Verified scenarios**: agent-with-override shows scoped list; agent-without-override shows defaults; unknown `agentId` falls back to defaults; empty per-agent `models` falls back to defaults; provider wildcards (`provider/*`) work in both global and per-agent maps.
- **Edge cases checked**: missing `agents.list` entirely, missing `models` key on an entry, empty object `models: {}`, mismatched `agentId`, mixed map (some agents override, others don't).
- **What I did NOT verify**: behavior on the TUI surface and any non-OpenClaw clients consuming the gateway protocol directly.

## Review Conversations

- [x] I will reply to or resolve every bot review conversation I address in this PR.
- [x] I will leave unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? **Yes**
- Config/env changes? **No** (new field is purely additive and optional)
- Migration needed? **No**

## Risks and Mitigations

- **Risk**: A user populates `agents.list[].models` expecting it to be additive on top of defaults.
  **Mitigation**: Documented in `docs/gateway/config-agents.md` that the per-agent map *replaces* the visible set for that agent (aliases and shared metadata are still inherited from defaults).

target.title: fix(gateway): disable startup trace monitor on startup failure
target.body:
## Summary
- add an explicit `startupTrace.close()` cleanup path in gateway startup tracing
- call `startupTrace.close()` in the startup failure catch path so `monitorEventLoopDelay` is always disabled on failed startup
- keep current extension type tests compiling after latest `main` changed stricter `Response` and regexp expectations

## Real behavior proof
- **Behavior or issue addressed**: Gateway startup tracing with `OPENCLAW_GATEWAY_STARTUP_TRACE=1` no longer leaves the event-loop delay monitor enabled when startup fails before readiness.
- **Real environment tested**: Local Linux checkout of this PR branch at `e81371fe6a0bbc8a84d5c1a51a7a821611f268fa`, running Node through the repo's `tsx` loader against a real temporary `OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH`.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**: Created a temporary invalid `openclaw.json`, then ran:
  ```sh
  OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH="$tmpdir/openclaw.json" OPENCLAW_GATEWAY_STARTUP_TRACE=1 timeout 20s node --import tsx -e 'import("./src/gateway/server.impl.ts").then(async ({ startGatewayServer }) => { try { await startGatewayServer(0, { bind: "loopback", controlUiEnabled: false }); console.log("unexpected startup success"); process.exitCode = 2; } catch (error) { console.log("startup failed as expected"); console.log(String(error instanceof Error ? error.message : error).split("\n")[0]); } })'
  ```
- **Evidence after fix**: Terminal output from the after-fix run:
  ```text
  [gateway] startup trace: config.snapshot.read.file 0.5ms total=26.7ms eventLoopMax=9.9ms
  [gateway] startup trace: config.snapshot.read.hash 0.2ms total=47.1ms eventLoopMax=0.0ms
  [gateway] startup trace: config.snapshot.read.parse 0.3ms total=49.3ms eventLoopMax=0.0ms
  [gateway] startup trace: config.snapshot.read 28.1ms total=53.1ms eventLoopMax=0.0ms
  [gateway] startup trace: config.snapshot 30.6ms total=55.1ms eventLoopMax=0.0ms
  startup failed as expected
  Invalid config at /tmp/openclaw-proof-4texI2/openclaw.json.
  ```
- **Observed result after fix**: The `node --import tsx` gateway startup process exited with status `0` under the `timeout 20s` wrapper after reporting the expected config failure, which shows the startup trace monitor did not keep the process alive after the failed startup path.
- **What was not tested**: Full packaged `openclaw gateway run` startup was not rerun because this source checkout does not contain built `dist/entry.js`; this proof directly imports the gateway server implementation from the PR checkout instead.

## Verification
- `corepack pnpm test src/gateway/server-channels.test.ts`
- `pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 src/gateway/server.impl.ts extensions/googlechat/src/targets.test.ts extensions/matrix/src/matrix/sdk/transport.test.ts`
- `git diff --check`
- `pnpm check:test-types`

PR surface: Source +51, Tests +187, Docs +24, Generated 0. Total +262 across 11 files.

Reproducibility: yes. at source level: current main emits `reply_to_bot` for replies to the bot and lets that implicit mention satisfy `allowBots="mentions"`. I did not run a fresh live Discord replay in this read-only review; the contributor supplied redacted live before/after output.

target.title: feat(discord): add implicit reply mention policy
target.body:
Closes #80234.

Adds `channels.discord.implicitReplyMentions`:

```json5
{
  fromUsers: true,
  fromBots: true
}
```

Defaults preserve current behavior. Multi-bot deployments can set `fromBots: false` so bot-authored native replies do not wake another bot via the implicit `reply_to_bot` path; explicit `<@botId>` mentions still work.

Checks run:

- `pnpm exec vitest run extensions/discord/src/monitor/message-handler.preflight.test.ts --maxWorkers=1`
- `pnpm config:schema:check`
- `pnpm config:docs:check`
- `pnpm check:bundled-channel-config-metadata`
- `git diff --check`

## Real behavior proof

Behavior or issue addressed: Discord native reply-only mentions can wake an OpenClaw bot when another bot replies to one of its messages. This PR adds a policy knob so deployments can keep human reply activation while disabling bot-authored reply-only activation.

Real environment tested: Private Discord server with a private test channel and two OpenClaw Discord bot accounts. Server, channel, bot names, message IDs, and account IDs are intentionally omitted from this public proof.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:
1. In the private test channel, send an anchor message from the target bot.
2. Send a native Discord reply from a separate relay bot to that anchor message. The reply body contains no textual `<@bot>` mention.
3. Observe whether the target bot wakes/responds.
4. Repeat after applying this branch with `implicitReplyMentions: { fromUsers: true, fromBots: false }`.
5. Send a separate control message from the relay bot that contains a real textual `<@bot>` mention and asks the target bot to reply with a known marker.

Evidence after fix: Redacted live output excerpt from the private Discord test channel:

```text
after-fix native reply-only test:
relay bot | "oc-proof-80235-after4-231018 native-reply-no-explicit-mention" | ref=<target-bot-anchor>
UNEXPECTED_TARGET_RESPONSES=0

explicit mention control:
target bot | "oc-proof-80235-control-231107 explicit-mention-ok" | ref=<relay-bot-control-message>
EXPLICIT_CONTROL_OK=true
```

Observed result after fix: With `fromBots:false`, the bot-authored native reply-only message did not wake the target bot during the observation window. A real textual explicit mention still woke the target bot and produced the requested marker response.

What was not tested: Human-authored reply-only behavior was covered by unit tests but not repeated in the live Discord proof. PluralKit behavior was covered by unit tests but not repeated in the live Discord proof.

target.title: fix(browser): time out remote tab enumeration
target.body:
## Summary
- Fixes #58968 by bounding remote CDP Playwright tab enumeration with the existing remote CDP timeout budget.
- Evicts the scoped cached/in-flight Playwright connection on enumeration timeout so the next request can reconnect instead of reusing a stuck pipe.
- Adds regression coverage for a stuck page-target lookup and verifies remote profile tab listing passes the resolved timeout into the Playwright path.

## Problem
Remote CDP profiles use a persistent Playwright connection for tab listing because HTTP `/json/list` is ephemeral for those providers. When that Playwright-backed enumeration stalls, browser requests can hang behind the stuck CDP operation instead of failing fast.

## Root Cause
`createProfileTabOps().readTabs()` passed only `cdpUrl` and SSRF policy to `listPagesViaPlaywright()`. The lower-level Playwright enumeration path had no operation-level timeout around `connectBrowser()`, `getAllPages()`, or per-page target/title/url reads, and a stuck scoped connection could remain cached or in-flight.

## Architectural Reasoning
The fix reuses the existing browser remote CDP timeout contract rather than adding a new config key. Remote tab listing now uses `max(remoteCdpTimeoutMs, remoteCdpHandshakeTimeoutMs)`, which matches the existing distinction between HTTP discovery and WebSocket handshake budgets. Timeout cleanup goes through the existing scoped Playwright disconnect helper, preserving per-CDP URL isolation and avoiding hidden coupling to other browser profiles.

## Real behavior proof
- **Behavior or issue addressed:** Remote CDP Playwright tab enumeration now returns a bounded timeout error instead of waiting indefinitely on a stalled CDP discovery/enumeration path.
- **Real environment tested:** Windows 11 worktree, Node `v22.15.0`, `pnpm 10.33.2`, branch `hemant/issue-triage-20260509`, commit `9aba9b405ae5c4601211fd0b2b428d9640425b8b`.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** Started a real loopback HTTP endpoint that accepts CDP discovery connections and deliberately never responds; called production `listPagesViaPlaywright()` through `node --import tsx` with `timeoutMs: 100` and an SSRF policy allowing loopback/private access; closed the scoped Playwright connection and destroyed the held socket after the call.
- **Evidence after fix:**

```console
$ node --import tsx - < hanging-cdp-smoke.mjs
{"ok":true,"error":"Playwright page enumeration timed out after 100ms","elapsedMs":309}
```

- **Observed result after fix:** The production browser module returned `Playwright page enumeration timed out after 100ms` and the script exited cleanly instead of hanging on the held CDP request.
- **What was not tested:** I did not use a real remote Chrome host because the failure mode is a stalled CDP endpoint; the local loopback endpoint exercises the same production timeout path with deterministic cleanup. Full `pnpm test extensions/browser` still has unrelated Windows environment failures noted below.

## Testing
- `pnpm test extensions/browser/src/browser/pw-session.connections.test.ts extensions/browser/src/browser/server-context.remote-profile-tab-ops.playwright.test.ts`
- `pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 extensions/browser/src/browser/pw-session.ts extensions/browser/src/browser/server-context.tab-ops.ts extensions/browser/src/browser/pw-session.connections.test.ts extensions/browser/src/browser/server-context.remote-profile-tab-ops.playwright.test.ts`
- `pnpm tsgo:extensions` from `O:\` subst drive to avoid the repo path-with-spaces launcher bug
- `pnpm tsgo:extensions:test` from `O:\`
- `pnpm lint:extensions` from `O:\`
- `pnpm check:changelog-attributions`
- `git diff --check upstream/main...HEAD`
- `pnpm check:changed --staged` exited 0 with no lane output

Full `pnpm test extensions/browser` was also run locally. It failed on unrelated Windows environment assumptions: local browser auto-detection / missing supported browser launch fixtures and existing `/tmp` path expectations being normalized to `C:\tmp`. The two touched regression test files pass.

## Risk Analysis
Risk is medium because this touches async browser/CDP lifecycle behavior. The change is scoped to remote Playwright tab enumeration, does not alter local managed browser JSON tab listing, and does not change navigation, action, or screenshot behavior. On timeout, OpenClaw drops only the scoped Playwright connection for that CDP URL and leaves the actual remote browser tabs intact.

## Backward Compatibility
No config, API, or response-shape changes. Existing `browser.remoteCdpTimeoutMs` and `browser.remoteCdpHandshakeTimeoutMs` now also bound remote Playwright tab enumeration.

## Screenshots / Logs
The real behavior proof above is terminal output from the after-fix smoke; no UI screenshot is applicable.

target.title: fix(gateway): add systemd watchdog heartbeat
target.body:
## Problem summary

Closes #11973. The generated systemd gateway unit had crash restart behavior, but it did not configure a watchdog heartbeat. If the gateway event loop wedged without exiting, systemd had no liveness signal to trigger recovery.

## Root cause

The existing gateway maintenance loop already emits a 30s keepalive tick, but that liveness signal only reaches OpenClaw clients. The systemd unit renderer did not opt into `WatchdogSec`, and the gateway runtime did not notify systemd through the `NOTIFY_SOCKET`/`systemd-notify` contract.

## Architectural reasoning

- Added a small `src/infra/systemd-notify.ts` helper that is inert unless `NOTIFY_SOCKET` is present, so non-systemd, Windows, macOS, foreground, and unmanaged gateway runs keep the same behavior.
- Reused `startGatewayMaintenanceTimers` as the watchdog cadence source. This ties the systemd ping to the existing event-loop tick instead of creating a separate interval or lifecycle path.
- Kept the systemd unit on the existing default `Type=simple` behavior and only added `WatchdogSec=90s` plus `NotifyAccess=all`. The `NotifyAccess=all` line is needed because notifications are sent by the `systemd-notify` helper process inside the service cgroup.
- The notifier disables itself after the first `systemd-notify` failure and logs once, avoiding repeated subprocess failures while preserving the gateway's existing session, cleanup, and health loops.
- No gateway protocol, API, session, or backward-compatibility contract changes.

## Testing performed

- `pnpm test src/infra/systemd-notify.test.ts src/gateway/server-maintenance.test.ts src/daemon/systemd-unit.test.ts` passed after rebase: 3 Vitest shards, 19 tests.
- `pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 src/infra/systemd-notify.ts src/infra/systemd-notify.test.ts src/gateway/server-maintenance.ts src/gateway/server-maintenance.test.ts src/daemon/systemd-unit.ts src/daemon/systemd-unit.test.ts docs/gateway/index.md docs/platforms/linux.md docs/vps.md CHANGELOG.md` passed.
- `pnpm lint:core` passed.
- `pnpm docs:check-mdx` passed.
- `pnpm lint:docs -- docs/gateway/index.md docs/platforms/linux.md docs/vps.md` passed.
- `pnpm tsgo:core` and `pnpm tsgo:core:test` passed from a subst drive path to avoid the local Windows path-with-space issue.
- `pnpm check:changed --staged` and `pnpm check:changed` exited 0 in this checkout, though they produced no lane output.

Known local environment limitations:

- Adding `src/daemon/systemd.test.ts` to the focused test command still shows pre-existing Windows-only failures unrelated to this change: `process.geteuid` is absent on Windows, and two env-file assertions expect an unquoted Windows path. The changed renderer test itself passes.
- `pnpm build` could not be completed in this Windows shell. The default `pnpm` wrapper is pinned to local Node v22.15.0 while the repo requires `>=22.16.0`; a subst-drive retry with bundled Node v24.14.0 gets past that but `build-all` still fails at the bootstrap guard because the expected generated `.js` dist aliases are missing in this environment. A direct `tsdown` build completed successfully, and generated hash drift was restored.

## Real behavior proof
- Behavior or issue addressed: Generated systemd gateway units now include watchdog recovery settings, and the runtime notifier is a safe no-op outside systemd-managed services.
- Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw checkout on Windows, branch `hemant/11973-systemd-watchdog`, Node v22.15.0 for focused proof commands.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: Ran `pnpm exec tsx` commands from the repo root to render the generated systemd unit and invoke the notifier with no `NOTIFY_SOCKET`.
- Evidence after fix (screenshot, recording, terminal capture, console output, redacted runtime log, linked artifact, or copied live output): Copied terminal output from the local run:

```console
$ pnpm exec tsx -e "import { buildSystemdUnit } from './src/daemon/systemd-unit.ts'; const unit = buildSystemdUnit({ description: 'OpenClaw Gateway', programArguments: ['/usr/bin/openclaw','gateway','run'], environment: {} }); console.log(unit.split('\n').filter((line) => ['Restart=always','RestartSec=5','WatchdogSec=90s','NotifyAccess=all','KillMode=control-group'].includes(line)).join('\n'));"
Restart=always
RestartSec=5
WatchdogSec=90s
NotifyAccess=all
KillMode=control-group

$ pnpm exec tsx -e "import { createSystemdNotifier } from './src/infra/systemd-notify.ts'; void (async () => { const notifier = createSystemdNotifier({ env: {} }); await notifier.ready(); await notifier.watchdog(); console.log('systemd notifier no-op without NOTIFY_SOCKET ok'); })();"
systemd notifier no-op without NOTIFY_SOCKET ok
```

- Observed result after fix: The actual unit renderer includes the watchdog lines, and the notifier performs no work when the systemd notification socket is absent. Unmanaged/local gateway runs remain unaffected, while managed systemd units can receive liveness pings from the existing maintenance tick.
- What was not tested: A live Linux systemd user service restart on a hung event loop from this Windows workspace.

## Risk analysis

Medium-low. The unit settings affect systemd-managed gateway installs only. Runtime notifications are gated by `NOTIFY_SOCKET`, failure-disabled after the first subprocess error, and do not change gateway protocol/session flows. The main operational risk is a missing `systemd-notify` binary on a systemd host; in that case the helper logs once and leaves existing crash-restart behavior intact.

## Screenshots/logs

No UI screenshots. This is CLI/service generation behavior; relevant command output is included above.

## Backward compatibility

Backward compatible. Existing unmanaged gateway runs, existing installed units until regenerated, non-systemd platforms, gateway API behavior, and session/gateway protocol behavior are unchanged. Docs now show the watchdog lines alongside the generated unit settings.

target.title: feat(tui,cli-runner): expose streamingWatchdogMs config + bump watchdog defaults
target.body:
# feat(tui,cli-runner): expose `streamingWatchdogMs` config + bump watchdog defaults

## Table of Contents
- [Summary](#Summary)
- [Motivation](#Motivation)
- [Changes](#Changes)
- [Test plan](#Test%20plan)
- [Backward compatibility](#Backward%20compatibility)

## Summary

- Plumbs `cli.tui.streamingWatchdogMs` through `runTui()` → `createEventHandlers({ streamingWatchdogMs })`. The consumer site at `src/tui/tui-event-handlers.ts:80` was already reading `context.streamingWatchdogMs` but no caller ever set it, so the in-source default fired unconditionally.
- Raises in-source defaults that were tripping prematurely on long-reasoning models, multi-minute tool calls, and SSE-keepalive gaps:
  - TUI streaming watchdog: 30s → 10m
  - CLI fresh-run watchdog ceiling: 10m → 60m
  - CLI resume watchdog ceiling: 3m → 60m

## Motivation

Operators running OpenClaw against long-reasoning models (Anthropic extended thinking, OpenAI reasoning chains) and slow tool runs were seeing the TUI report `idle` and surface the "This response is taking longer than expected. Send another message to continue." prompt **mid-run, while the model was still thinking** — because the 30s streaming-delta watchdog was both (a) too short for modern models and (b) had no config knob.

The same operators were also seeing CLI runs aborted by the no-output watchdog at the 10-minute / 3-minute ceilings (fresh / resume). Per-backend overrides at `cli.watchdog.fresh.maxMs` were already supported, but the in-source defaults left every operator who hadn't tuned per-backend exposed.

This PR closes both gaps in one stroke: exposes the TUI streaming-watchdog as a first-class config knob, and lifts the CLI ceilings to a value (60m) that matches the longest legitimate run we've observed in production while still allowing per-backend overrides to dial it back.

## Changes

| File | Change |
|---|---|
| `src/tui/tui-event-handlers.ts` | `DEFAULT_STREAMING_WATCHDOG_MS`: 30_000 → 600_000 (with explanatory comment) |
| `src/tui/tui.ts` | New exported helper `resolveTuiStreamingWatchdogMs(config)`; wired into the existing `createEventHandlers({...})` call inside `runTui` |
| `src/config/types.cli.ts` | New `CliTuiConfig` type with optional `streamingWatchdogMs?: number`; added under `CliConfig.tui` |
| `src/config/zod-schema.ts` | Added strict `cli.tui.streamingWatchdogMs: z.number().int().nonnegative().optional()` |
| `src/agents/cli-watchdog-defaults.ts` | `CLI_FRESH_WATCHDOG_DEFAULTS.maxMs`: 600_000 → 3_600_000; `CLI_RESUME_WATCHDOG_DEFAULTS.maxMs`: 180_000 → 3_600_000 |
| `src/tui/tui.test.ts` | 5 new unit tests for `resolveTuiStreamingWatchdogMs` (unset, happy, zero, fractional, hostile inputs) |
| `src/tui/tui-event-handlers.test.ts` | 1 new test asserting the new 10m default fires past 600s but not at 31s |
| `docs/gateway/configuration-reference.md` | Documented the new `cli.tui.streamingWatchdogMs` knob in the existing `## CLI` section |

## Test plan

```bash
pnpm install
pnpm build:strict-smoke
pnpm vitest run --config test/vitest/vitest.tui.config.ts \
  src/tui/tui-event-handlers.test.ts src/tui/tui.test.ts
pnpm vitest run --config test/vitest/vitest.agents-core.config.ts \
  src/agents/cli-runner.reliability.test.ts
pnpm vitest run --config test/vitest/vitest.runtime-config.config.ts \
  src/config/schema.test.ts
pnpm tsgo:test
pnpm lint
```

Manual verification:

1. `openclaw configure` → set `cli.tui.streamingWatchdogMs: 60000` → run a long-reasoning prompt → confirm the "taking longer" notice fires at 60s.
2. Remove the override → confirm it now fires at 10 minutes (not 30s).
3. Set `cli.tui.streamingWatchdogMs: 0` → confirm the watchdog is disabled.

## Backward compatibility

- `cli.tui.streamingWatchdogMs` is **optional**. Configs that don't set it pick up the new in-source default of 10 minutes (was 30 seconds). This is a user-facing default change, justified above; flagged in the changelog.
- `CLI_*_WATCHDOG_DEFAULTS.maxMs` changes only affect callers that did not set their own per-backend `cli.watchdog.fresh.maxMs` / `.resume.maxMs`. The existing `resolveCliNoOutputTimeoutMs` test at `src/agents/cli-runner.reliability.test.ts:999` still passes because its computed value (480000 = 600000 * 0.8) sits below both the old and new ceilings.
- No schema removals, no field renames, no deprecations. Pure addition + default-tuning.

target.title: feat: add per-agent and per-cron-job provider request headers
target.body:
## Summary

🤖 **AI-assisted PR**

Closes #80026.

- Add `providers` field to agent config (`agents.list[].providers.<provider>.request`) for per-agent provider request overrides (custom headers, auth, proxy, TLS, allowPrivateNetwork)
- Add `providers` to cron job payload (`payload.providers.<provider>.request.headers`) for per-job header overrides only — auth, proxy, TLS, and allowPrivateNetwork are not exposed through mutable cron payloads
- Three-level precedence: global `models.providers` → agent-level → cron job-level
- Agent-level overrides work even without global provider config (discovered built-in providers)
- Job-level overrides work for default-agent cron jobs without `agents.list` entries
- `providers` persists through cron payload update/patch operations
- Cron job merge path deep-merges headers, preserving non-conflicting agent-level headers
- 18 new tests covering agent-only, three-way merge, cron patch persistence

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior or issue addressed**: Per-agent and per-cron-job provider request headers are injected into outbound LLM provider HTTP requests. Three-level merge (global → agent → job) with later levels overriding earlier ones on conflict. Agent-level overrides work for discovered providers without global config. Cron job overrides are restricted to headers only.

**Real environment tested**: Node 24.13.0, openclaw v2026.5.8, Linux x86_64. Mock HTTP server on localhost captures and logs all incoming request headers. Uses the actual OpenClaw source code (`resolveAgentProviderRequest`, `mergeModelProviderRequestOverrides`, `resolveProviderRequestConfig`, `attachModelProviderRequestTransport`) via tsx.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch**:
1. Started a mock HTTP server on a random localhost port that logs all incoming request headers and returns a valid OpenAI-compatible chat completion response
2. Ran a Node script that imports the actual OpenClaw provider-request-config module and:
   - Resolved agent-level headers from config with `resolveAgentProviderRequest` (no global provider config)
   - Built a three-level merge of global → agent → job headers with `mergeModelProviderRequestOverrides`
   - Resolved the final request config with `resolveProviderRequestConfig`
   - Sent an HTTP POST to the mock server with the merged headers
3. Verified the mock server received all headers with correct values and precedence

**Evidence after fix**:
```
Mock provider listening on http://127.0.0.1:42657/v1

Agent-only (no global provider config):
  Agent-level provider request: {
  "headers": {
    "X-Proof-Header": "agent-level-custom",
    "X-Cache-Control": "no-cache"
  }
}

Three-level merge (global → agent → job):
  Global:  { "X-Global": "global-val", "X-Override": "from-global" }
  Agent:   { "X-Override": "from-agent", "X-Agent": "agent-val" }
  Job:     { "X-Job": "from-job", "X-Override": "from-job" }
  Merged:  { "X-Global": "global-val", "X-Override": "from-job", "X-Agent": "agent-val", "X-Job": "from-job" }

  PASS: Agent-only headers resolve without global config
  PASS: Global headers preserved in merge
  PASS: Agent-only headers preserved in merge
  PASS: Job-level headers present in merge
  PASS: Job-level overrides agent-level on conflict

=== Captured request on mock server ===
URL: /v1/chat/completions
Relevant headers: {
  'x-global': 'global-val',
  'x-agent': 'agent-val',
  'x-job': 'from-job',
  'x-override': 'from-job',
}

  PASS: X-Global arrived at server
  PASS: X-Agent arrived at server
  PASS: X-Job arrived at server
  PASS: X-Override has job-level value at server

=== All end-to-end proof checks passed ===
```

**Observed result after fix**: All custom headers configured at agent and job level arrive at the provider server with correct values. The three-level merge correctly applies precedence: job-level overrides agent-level, agent-level overrides global, and non-conflicting headers from all levels are preserved. Agent-only headers (no global provider config) resolve and attach correctly. All 9 proof assertions pass.

**What was not tested**: Live provider request through the full OpenClaw gateway (requires API key and real provider). The mock server proves the headers flow through the same code path (`resolveProviderRequestConfig` → HTTP fetch) that the gateway uses.

## Test plan
- [x] `pnpm test provider-request-config` — 30/30 tests pass
- [x] `pnpm test pi-embedded-runner/model` — 79/79 tests pass
- [x] `pnpm test service.jobs` — 59/59 tests pass
- [x] `pnpm build` — passes
- [x] `pnpm check:changed` — core + core tests lanes pass (extension lane has pre-existing jsonc-parser issue unrelated to this PR)
- [x] End-to-end: mock server confirms custom headers arrive at provider with correct three-level precedence

target.title: feat(tools): introduce group:core for all built-in core tools
target.body: ## Summary - Problem: `group:openclaw` is documented as "all built-in tools" but actually filters out filesystem and shell runtime tools, so there is no obvious `group:*` shorthand for "all built-in core tools." - Why it matters: operators writing `tools.allow: ["group:openclaw"]` expecting broad built-in access get a curated subset instead. A dedicated all-core group name removes the ambiguity. - What changed: added `group:core` as the all-built-in-core-tools shorthand; updated `group:openclaw` docs to accurately say "curated OpenClaw integration tools (excludes filesystem, shell runtime, and provider plugins)"; added `expandToolGroups` to the plugin allowlist normalizer so `group:*` entries work in plugin tool policy. - What did NOT change (scope boundary): no profile semantics changed. `tools.profile: "full"` still means unrestricted, same as unset. Plugin tools are still not included by `group:core`. ## Change Type (select all) - [x] Bug fix - [ ] Feature - [x] Refactor required for the fix - [x] Docs - [ ] Security hardening - [ ] Chore/infra ## Scope (select all touched areas) - [x] Gateway / orchestration - [x] Skills / tool execution - [ ] Auth / tokens - [ ] Memory / storage - [ ] Integrations - [ ] API / contracts - [x] UI / DX - [ ] CI/CD / infra ## Linked Issue/PR - Closes # - Related # - [ ] This PR fixes a bug or regression ## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs) - Behavior or issue addressed: My OpenClaw runs in a non-admin account with it's own credentials. As such when configuring the browser plugin, I discovered that it had to be explicitly added to tools.allow and that I could allow all future configured plugins with `group:plugins` ```bash openclaw config set tools.allow '["group:plugins"]' --strict-json ``` However, when I did this my agent lost access to all core tools because if tools.allow is present it must specify everything allowed. So I went looking for a group similar to group:plugins and found it missing. Now, with this change full yolo looks like this. ```bash openclaw config set agents.defaults.sandbox.mode off openclaw config set tools.profile full openclaw config set tools.exec.ask off openclaw exec-policy preset yolo openclaw config set tools.allow '["group:core","group:plugins"]' --strict-json ``` - Real environment tested: Mac Mini with openclaw v2026.5.2 built from source ```bash git checkout v2026.5.2 git apply seank-group-core.patch pnpm install pnpm ui:build pnpm build ./scripts/package-mac-app.sh pnpm test src/agents/tool-policy.test.ts src/agents/pi-tools.create-openclaw-coding-tools.test.ts src/plugins/tools.optional.test.ts pnpm link --global ``` - Exact steps or command run after this patch: ```bash openclaw config set tools.allow '["group:core","group:plugins"]' --strict-json openclaw gateway restart ``` - Evidence after fix (screenshot): - Observed result after fix: OpenClaw had access to all core tools as well as browser plugin - What was not tested: I only ran this live on the environment above, beside that just unittest suggested by OpenClaw and GitHub Copilot - Before evidence (optional but encouraged): ## Root Cause (if applicable) - Root cause: `group:openclaw` was both the only broad group name and a curated subset, creating a naming gap where no group meant "all built-in core tools." - Missing detection / guardrail: no test asserted the existence of an all-core group or verified that `group:openclaw` excluded fs/runtime tools. - Contributing context (if known): the naming collision was a design clarity problem, not a runtime regression. ## Regression Test Plan (if applicable) - Coverage level that should have caught this: - [x] Unit test - [ ] Seam / integration test - [ ] End-to-end test - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient - Target test or file: - `src/agents/tool-policy.test.ts` - `src/agents/pi-tools.create-openclaw-coding-tools.test.ts` - `src/plugins/tools.optional.test.ts` - Scenario the test should lock in: - `group:core` expands to built-in core tools. - Plugin optional tools are not enabled by `group:core` alone, but still work with `group:plugins`. - Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: the behavior is pure policy expansion, so unit coverage around the catalog, global tool policy, and plugin allowlist boundary is sufficient. - Existing test that already covers this (if any): existing group expansion tests covered section groups and `group:openclaw`; this PR adds explicit coverage for `group:core`. - If no new test is added, why not: N/A ## User-visible / Behavior Changes - `tools.allow` / `tools.deny` now support `group:core` for all built-in core tools. - Docs now distinguish: - `tools.profile: "full"`: unrestricted profile, same as unset. - `group:core`: all built-in core tools, excluding plugin tools. - `group:openclaw`: curated OpenClaw integration tools, excluding filesystem, shell runtime, and plugin tools. ## Diagram (if applicable) ```text Before: [user wants broad built-in tool access] -> group:openclaw -> curated subset (misleading docs say "all") After: [user wants all built-in core tools] -> group:core -> all built-in core tools [user wants curated integration tools] -> group:openclaw -> curated subset (docs now accurate) [user wants unrestricted profile] -> tools.profile: full -> unrestricted baseline ``` ## Security Impact (required) - New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`): No - Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`): No - New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`): No - Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`): Yes - Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`): No - If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: `group:core` is an explicit allow/deny shorthand for existing built-in tools. It does not grant anything unless an operator opts into it in tool policy. Tests lock that it does not enable optional plugin tools by itself. ## Repro + Verification ### Environment - OS: Windows 11 (rebase + verification); macOS Darwin 25.4.0 arm64 (original patch) - Runtime/container: Node 22, local OpenClaw repo - Model/provider: N/A - Integration/channel (if any): N/A - Relevant config (redacted): tool policy allowlist examples using `group:core` and `group:plugins` ### Steps 1. Configure `tools.allow: ["group:core"]`. 2. Resolve OpenClaw coding tools. 3. Verify built-in core tools such as `read`, `exec`, `canvas`, `message`, and `cron` are available. 4. Verify optional plugin tools are not enabled by `group:core` alone. ### Expected - `group:core` expands to built-in core tools. - Plugin tools still require explicit plugin/tool allowlist entries or `group:plugins`. ### Actual - Matches expected. ## Evidence - [x] Failing test/log before + passing after - [ ] Trace/log snippets - [ ] Screenshot/recording - [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant) Tests after rebase onto current `upstream/main` (59326c8e3b): ```sh pnpm test src/agents/tool-policy.test.ts src/agents/pi-tools.create-openclaw-coding-tools.test.ts src/plugins/tools.optional.test.ts ``` ```text ✓ unit-fast src/agents/tool-policy.test.ts (32 tests) 23ms ✓ agents src/agents/pi-tools.create-openclaw-coding-tools.test.ts (55 tests) 779ms ✓ plugins src/plugins/tools.optional.test.ts (62 tests) 3773ms Test Files 3 passed (3) Tests 149 passed (149) ``` ## Human Verification (required) What you personally verified (not just CI), and how: - Verified scenarios: - Live gateway with `tools.allow: ["group:core"]` in agent config. - `group:core` is present in the core tool group map (`src/agents/tool-catalog.ts`). - Global allowlist expansion accepts `group:core`. - Optional plugin tools are not unlocked by `group:core` alone. - Docs list `group:core` and no longer describe `group:openclaw` as all built-in tools. - `expandToolGroups` correctly wired into plugin allowlist normalizer (`src/plugins/tools.ts`). - Edge cases checked: - `group:core` plus `group:plugins` still allows optional plugin tools. - Stale group naming references searched across `docs`, `src`, `packages`, and `README.md`. - `group:core` confirmed absent from `upstream/main` before this patch (not previously landed). - What you did **not** verify: - Full CI. - Rendered docs site. ## Review Conversations - [ ] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR. - [ ] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment. If a bot review conversation is addressed by this PR, resolve that conversation yourself. Do not leave bot review conversation cleanup for maintainers. ## Compatibility / Migration - Backward compatible? (`Yes/No`): Yes - Config/env changes? (`Yes/No`): Yes, `group:core` is a new documented tool-policy shorthand. - Migration needed? (`Yes/No`): No - If yes, exact upgrade steps: N/A ## Risks and Mitigations - Risk: users might assume `group:core` includes plugin tools. - Mitigation: docs state plugin tools are excluded, and tests verify optional plugins require `group:plugins` or explicit plugin/tool allowlist entries. - Risk: `group:openclaw` documentation could imply broader access than it actually provides. - Mitigation: docs now call it a curated integration group and explicitly distinguish it from `group:core`. ## Attribution - **OpenClaw (Codex 5.5)**: identified the naming gap, proposed the fix, authored the original commit and initial PR description. - **GitHub Copilot (Claude Opus 4.6)**: verified the fix was still viable on current `main`, rebased the commit (resolving 3 merge conflicts including an `expandToolGroups` import relocation to `tool-policy-shared.ts`), ran tests, and rewrote the PR description to match the current template.

target.title: feat(gateway): allow chat.inject to override provider attribution via originAgent
target.body:
## Summary

Adds an optional `originAgent` field to `ChatInjectParamsSchema` and persists it as a **separate, dedicated attribution field** on the injected assistant transcript entry. The pre-existing `(provider: "openclaw", model: "gateway-injected")` sentinel pair is **preserved unchanged**, so the runtime replay filter at `src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/replay-history.ts:271` continues to correctly drop these rows from being replayed back to the next model call.

UI avatar/handle resolvers can key off the new persisted `originAgent` field to render the true source of a cross-agent inject (e.g. Hermes, Codex) instead of always showing the OpenClaw avatar.

## Why

`chat.inject` is the canonical RPC for cross-agent transcript writes. Today the persisted entry has `provider: "openclaw"` hard-coded, with `additionalProperties: false` on the schema closing the door on any caller-supplied attribution. When external agents (Hermes, Codex, Claude Code, etc.) inject via this RPC, the message renders with OpenClaw's avatar and the only visible attribution is whatever the caller embeds in body text. This breaks the "who said what" guarantee in long transcripts.

Discovered in production by an external agent (Hermes) attempting a cross-agent verify ping into a webchat session. The body-tag workaround ("FROM: Hermes —") works at-a-glance but degrades attribution clarity over time and across many entries.

## Behavior

- **With `originAgent` set:** persisted entry gets a new `originAgent: "<value>"` field. `provider` and `model` are unchanged.
- **Without `originAgent`:** behavior is byte-identical to current main. No new field is written. Fully backward compatible.
- **Empty / whitespace-only input:** schema rejects literal `""` (`minLength: 1`); persistence helper additionally trims whitespace and treats blanks as absent, so a malformed value never lands on disk even if a future schema change loosens validation.

## Why a separate field, not provider override

The Codex review on the prior commit caught a real correctness defect: `provider` is part of the runtime replay contract, not just display. `normalizeAssistantReplayContent` (`src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/replay-history.ts:271`) drops gateway-injected rows from model replay only when `provider === "openclaw"` and the model is one of `{ "delivery-mirror", "gateway-injected" }`. Overwriting `provider` would have caused transcript-only injects to leak into model context, duplicating content and risking provider ordering rejections.

The redesigned patch keeps the sentinel pair untouched and adds attribution as a dedicated parallel field. UI consumers read the new field; the replay filter is unaffected.

## Security

`chat.inject` is already gated at operator-privilege scope (`src/gateway/method-scopes.ts`). Anyone who can call it can already write arbitrary assistant content into a session transcript. `originAgent` is **purely presentational** and introduces no new impersonation surface beyond what current authorized callers already possess. Because the sentinel pair is preserved, the previously-flagged security risk (UI-only injects entering model replay) does not apply to this revision.

## Files

- `src/gateway/protocol/schema/logs-chat.ts` — schema field with `minLength: 1`, `maxLength: 100`
- `src/gateway/server-methods/chat.ts` — handler param + helper threading
- `src/gateway/server-methods/chat-transcript-inject.ts` — new persisted field, sentinel preserved, trim+empty normalization
- `apps/shared/OpenClawKit/Sources/OpenClawProtocol/GatewayModels.swift` — regenerated via `pnpm protocol:gen:swift`

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior or issue addressed:** Cross-agent `chat.inject` calls render with the OpenClaw avatar in the operator's webchat UI because the persisted assistant entry has no caller-controlled attribution field. External agents currently have no way to mark the inject as their own without putting "FROM: Hermes —" inline in body text, which degrades transcript clarity. This patch adds a dedicated `originAgent` persisted field while preserving the `(provider: "openclaw", model: "gateway-injected")` sentinel that the replay-history filter relies on.

**Real environment tested:** Sandboxed checkout of openclaw main at `/tmp/openclaw-pr/openclaw-fresh` with the patched files and the regenerated protocol bindings. Used the actual openclaw runtime — `appendInjectedAssistantMessageToTranscript` and `validateChatInjectParams` are invoked via `node` through `tsx` against synthetic transcript fixtures created with the project's own `createTranscriptFixtureSync` helper. No mocks, no stubs, no real session keys, no production data.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch:**

```
node node_modules/.bin/tsx scripts/proof-chat-inject-origin-agent.mts
node node_modules/.bin/tsx scripts/proof-chat-inject-schema.mts
```

Both scripts are committed under `scripts/` in this PR so a maintainer can rerun them.

**Evidence after fix:** Live stdout captured from the runtime invocations above. Persistence-layer output shows the actual `provider`, `model`, and `originAgent` fields read back from the jsonl transcript file on disk after the patched code wrote to it. Schema-layer output is the AJV validator's verdict on real input shapes.

Persistence-layer terminal output (live runtime, the patched code path actually executed):

```
OpenClaw chat.inject originAgent attribution — real-behavior proof
Patched modules under test:
  src/gateway/server-methods/chat-transcript-inject.ts
  src/gateway/protocol/schema/logs-chat.ts (schema field)
  src/gateway/server-methods/chat.ts (handler threading)

Design:
  provider stays 'openclaw' (replay-filter sentinel preserved)
  originAgent is a NEW persisted field for display-only attribution
  empty/whitespace originAgent is normalized to absent

=================================================
CASE                  : baseline-no-origin
input.originAgent     : <unset>
append.ok             : true
messageId             : 09641925-2611-4401-86c6-d369a045b518
stamped.provider      : openclaw
stamped.model         : gateway-injected
stamped.originAgent   : <absent>
expected.originAgent  : absent
result                : PASS

=================================================
CASE                  : hermes
input.originAgent     : "hermes"
append.ok             : true
messageId             : fcd260be-a790-489b-82e5-f393d136e321
stamped.provider      : openclaw
stamped.model         : gateway-injected
stamped.originAgent   : hermes
expected.originAgent  : hermes
result                : PASS

=================================================
CASE                  : codex
input.originAgent     : "codex"
append.ok             : true
messageId             : ef02a786-0e71-48d3-aafb-b861049d8301
stamped.provider      : openclaw
stamped.model         : gateway-injected
stamped.originAgent   : codex
expected.originAgent  : codex
result                : PASS

=================================================
CASE                  : blank-empty-string
input.originAgent     : ""
append.ok             : true
messageId             : 9943b96b-9bfc-4050-a997-b362c10e2422
stamped.provider      : openclaw
stamped.model         : gateway-injected
stamped.originAgent   : <absent>
expected.originAgent  : absent
result                : PASS

=================================================
CASE                  : whitespace-only
input.originAgent     : "   "
append.ok             : true
messageId             : 2aa2899d-a2f7-4679-a404-4236009f8c79
stamped.provider      : openclaw
stamped.model         : gateway-injected
stamped.originAgent   : <absent>
expected.originAgent  : absent
result                : PASS

=================================================
CASE                  : trim-surrounding-whitespace
input.originAgent     : "  hermes  "
append.ok             : true
messageId             : 4b1f86b7-060d-4cce-a81a-0cfcfb70ddca
stamped.provider      : openclaw
stamped.model         : gateway-injected
stamped.originAgent   : hermes
expected.originAgent  : hermes
result                : PASS
```

Schema-layer terminal output (live AJV validator on patched schema):

```
ChatInjectParamsSchema validator behavior:

---
CASE     : legacy params (sessionKey + message + label)
accepted : true
---
CASE     : patched params (with non-empty originAgent)
accepted : true
---
CASE     : rejects empty-string originAgent (minLength:1)
accepted : false
errors   : [{"instancePath":"/originAgent","schemaPath":"#/properties/originAgent/minLength","keyword":"minLength","params":{"limit":1},"message":"must NOT have fewer than 1 characters"}]
---
CASE     : rejects unknown extra fields (additionalProperties:false preserved)
accepted : false
errors   : [{"instancePath":"","schemaPath":"#/additionalProperties","keyword":"additionalProperties","params":{"additionalProperty":"somethingMadeUp"},"message":"must NOT have additional properties"}]
```

**Observed result after fix:**

- Baseline call (no `originAgent`) → persisted entry has `provider: "openclaw"`, `model: "gateway-injected"`, no `originAgent` field. Byte-identical to current-main behavior; replay filter still matches.
- Patched call (`originAgent: "hermes"`) → persisted entry has `provider: "openclaw"`, `model: "gateway-injected"`, **and** a new `originAgent: "hermes"` field. Replay filter still matches the sentinel pair and drops the row; UI consumers can attribute it to Hermes via the new field.
- Patched call (`originAgent: "codex"`) → same as above with `originAgent: "codex"`. Confirms the field is not Hermes-specific.
- Blank input (`""` or whitespace-only) → persisted entry has no `originAgent` field. The persistence helper trims and normalizes, so malformed input cannot land on disk.
- Trimmed input (`"  hermes  "`) → persisted entry has `originAgent: "hermes"` (whitespace-stripped).
- Schema validator accepts non-empty `originAgent`, rejects literal `""` via the new `minLength: 1`, and continues to reject unknown extra fields via the unchanged `additionalProperties: false`.

**What was not tested:** End-to-end UI render in the actual webchat surface (visually confirming a Hermes avatar/handle in the live React UI keyed off the new `originAgent` field) is intentionally out of scope for this PR — the patch only adds the persisted attribution field; UI consumption is a follow-up that will pick up the field from the transcript. The replay-filter regression that the prior PR revision would have introduced is now structurally impossible because the sentinel pair is unchanged; no run-time replay test was added because the contract being preserved already has coverage in `src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/replay-history.test.ts`.

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)
